title: Effortless Task Management with Customization Challenges
reviewText: I love how Jira allows me to manage project planning and task management effectively with different boards for various teams and workflows, whether it's Scrum or Kanban. My long-term project planning board is great as it provides a single overview of all current projects, and I can drill down into details easily. Similarly, my day-to-day task planning board is very straightforward, as I can quickly create cards for specific tasks. I like that I can handle a lot of tasks without leaving the main project or task overview, such as creating new cards, dragging them, changing titles, and assigning ownership without navigating multiple dialog boxes. Card management feels very frictionless, allowing me to create tasks during a meeting seamlessly. It's easy to set up a board, and everything falls into place within minutes, enabling me to start creating tasks quickly. I find the customization options in Jira to be quite complex. While the board templates work fine for my needs, setting up custom workflows can be challenging to get them exactly how you want. They could improve this by including frequently used custom workflows as part of the default offering. I use Jira to keep a clear overview of project statuses and manage day-to-day tasks seamlessly, ensuring I never forget anything. title: Intuitive and Effective for Team Coordination
reviewText: I really like the UI of Jira. I've used several other tools, and Jira is my favorite because of how intuitive it is to use. It's very easy to navigate. title: Great Ticket Tracking with Rich Comments in Jira
reviewText: In my role as a Data Engineer, we use Jira every day to manage sprint tasks, track bugs, and drive feature development. I mainly work in Scrum boards, where tickets move through stages like To Do, In Progress, Code Review, and Done, which gives the team clear visibility into the full development lifecycle. One feature I find particularly helpful is the ability to add detailed comments and include supporting context such as logs, screenshots, and links. For instance, when I’m working on ETL pipelines, I can attach error logs and query outputs directly to the ticket, which makes it easier for others to reproduce issues and helps the team debug faster. Jira’s epic-and-story structure also makes it easier to break down larger data engineering work into smaller, manageable pieces. As a result, sprint planning feels more organized, and tracking progress throughout the sprint is much more efficient. Jira is very useful, but for new users it can feel a bit complex at first. Also, finding specific tickets can sometimes take extra time, especially when filters aren’t set up properly. Jira helps me handle blockers and manage dependencies between teams. For instance, if I run into a permission or access issue while working with data sources or AWS services, I create a ticket and assign it to the appropriate team. This ensures the issue is properly tracked, visible to everyone involved, and resolved as quickly as possible. It also keeps everything in one place—updates, comments, and task progress—which makes communication smoother and reduces back-and-forth. Overall, it helps us stay aligned during sprints and complete our work on time. Great Ticket Tracking with Rich Comments in Jira

Jira can feel overly complex and slow when workflows, custom fields, or large backlogs pile up, and the reporting/admin settings are often harder to navigate than they should be. The main improvement I'd make is to stop Jira from turning ordinary workflow setup into a small administrative archaeology project - Jira gets cluttered, requires too much admin help, and slows down with large backlogs, complex workflows, and heavy customization. The initial setup was reasonably straightforward at the surface, but Jira does have a charming talent for making 'basic setup' turn into a mini configuration exercise once you start adjusting workflows, permissions, and fields. It's a strong tool for organization, workflow tracking, and cross-team visibility, but the complexity, admin overhead, and occasional 'why is this harder than it needs to be?' moments keep it from being an effortless recommendation. title: Excellent for Managing Projects, Teams, and Products Across the Corporation
reviewText: Jira holds the whole company in one place — engineering, design, QA, and ops all working off the same backlog. For us, that's the real value. No more "where does this ticket live" or chasing status in Slack. A few things we use every day: custom workflows that match how each team actually works, sprints with proper velocity tracking, and the issue hierarchy (epics → stories → subtasks) that lets leadership see the roadmap and engineers see today's work in the same tool. Automation rules quietly do the boring stuff — moving tickets, pinging owners, closing stale items — and save us hours a week. The Slack and GitHub integrations are also a big deal. A PR linked to a ticket updates status automatically, so reporting takes care of itself. Slow and might be buggy depends on the use caseJira can feel heavy. Loading a busy board or running a JQL query on a large project is noticeably slow, and the UI has gotten more cluttered with every release — too many nested menus and panels for what should be simple actions. Configuration is the other one. Out of the box, Jira tries to be everything for everyone, and getting it set up properly for your team takes a real admin investment. Permissions, screens, custom fields, workflows — every change feels like it needs a project of its own. Smaller teams without a dedicated admin end up either over-configuring or giving up on half the features. Pricing also adds up fast once you layer in Confluence, advanced roadmaps, and the marketplace plugins most teams actually need. A leaner, faster, more opinionated default experience would go a long way. Before Jira, our roadmap lived in spreadsheets, priorities lived in someone's head, and progress lived in standup notes. Engineering, product, and design were all working off slightly different versions of the truth — and small misalignments turned into shipped bugs and missed deadlines. Now everything is in one place. Every initiative has an epic, every epic breaks down into stories, and every story has an owner and a status anyone in the company can see. Sprints give us a real cadence. Reporting takes minutes instead of hours — burn-down, velocity, and release health are right there. The concrete wins: planning meetings are shorter, leadership stopped asking "what's the status of X" because they can see it themselves, and our release cycle has tightened by a sprint. For a product team that ships every two weeks, that compounds fast. Excellent for Managing Projects, Teams, and Products Across the Corporation

title: Powerful Bug Tracking and Project Management Tool for QA Teams
reviewText: Jira is an outstanding tool for QA and development teams. The customizable workflows allow me to create tailored defect management processes that match our testing lifecycle. I particularly appreciate the ability to create custom issue types for bugs, test cases, and improvement tickets. The sprint board and backlog management make it easy to prioritize testing tasks, and the integration with tools like Selenium and CI/CD pipelines streamlines our automation workflows. The JQL (Jira Query Language) is extremely powerful for filtering and reporting on test coverage and defect metrics. Bulk editing and linking related issues saves significant time during regression cycles. The performance can be sluggish when dealing with large projects that have thousands of issues. Page load times for complex dashboards and filters can be frustrating during busy sprint periods. The configuration complexity is steep - setting up advanced workflows, permission schemes, and notification schemes requires significant admin expertise. The pricing model has also become increasingly expensive, especially for smaller teams. Additionally, the mobile app lacks many features available in the desktop version, making it difficult to manage tickets on the go. Some of the new UI changes in the cloud version feel unintuitive compared to the server version. Before using Jira, our QA team struggled with scattered bug reports across emails and spreadsheets, making it nearly impossible to track defect status and testing progress across sprints. Jira has completely transformed how we manage our testing lifecycle. We can now log, prioritize, and track every defect with detailed steps to reproduce, screenshots, and severity levels. The workflow automation routes bugs directly to the responsible developers and notifies the QA team when fixes are ready for re-testing. Our defect leakage rate has reduced significantly, and test cycle times have improved by nearly 30% due to better visibility and collaboration. Real-time dashboards give stakeholders immediate insight into release readiness and quality metrics. Powerful Bug Tracking and Project Management Tool for QA Teams

title: Flexible and Easy to Use, but Can Get Complex
reviewText: What I like best about Jira is how it brings structure and clarity to complex workflows while still being highly customizable. On the UI/UX side, the Scrum and Kanban boards with drag-and-drop make task tracking very intuitive. I’ve personally found that organizing tasks into sprints and visually moving them across stages helps reduce confusion and saves time during daily standups. Integrations are another strong point. Jira connects smoothly with tools like GitHub and Slack, which means updates from commits, pull requests, and team discussions are all linked in one place. This reduces context switching and keeps the whole team aligned without extra effort. In terms of performance, it handles large projects reliably, and I rarely face major slowdowns even with many issues and users. For pricing and ROI, while it might feel a bit expensive for small teams at first, the time saved through automation, organized workflows, and better team coordination makes it worth the cost. It reduces manual tracking and improves overall productivity, which directly impacts delivery speed. Support and onboarding are also solid. There’s extensive documentation, templates, and community resources that make it easier to get started, even if you’re new to project management tools. Regarding AI and intelligence, Jira has started introducing smart features like automation suggestions and predictive insights, which help in prioritizing tasks and reducing repetitive work. Even simple automation rules (like auto-assigning or status updates) have a noticeable impact on efficiency. Overall, Jira stands out because it combines flexibility, strong integrations, and intelligent automation to improve workflow and team collaboration in a very practical way. One of the main things I dislike about Jira is that the initial setup and customization can be overwhelming, especially for new users. While the flexibility is powerful, configuring workflows, permissions, and issue types often requires time and experience, which can slow down onboarding. From a UI/UX perspective, the interface can feel cluttered at times, particularly when dealing with large projects or heavily customized boards. It’s not always intuitive to find certain settings, and this can impact productivity until you get used to it. In terms of performance, Jira can occasionally become slow when handling very large datasets or complex filters, which affects the overall experience during peak usage. Integrations are strong, but managing them can sometimes be tricky. For example, syncing with tools like GitHub or Slack may require additional configuration or troubleshooting to get everything working smoothly. Regarding pricing and ROI, it might not be the most cost-effective option for small teams or startups, especially when adding multiple users or premium features. The cost can increase quickly as the team scales. Support and onboarding are decent, but resolving specific or advanced issues sometimes depends heavily on documentation or community forums rather than direct support. As for AI and intelligence, while there are some useful automation features, the AI capabilities still feel somewhat limited compared to what could be expected. More advanced insights or smarter recommendations could further improve productivity. Overall, Jira is very powerful, but its complexity, occasional performance issues, and pricing structure can be challenging depending on the team size and experience level. Jira solves the problem of scattered task management and lack of visibility in team workflows. Instead of tracking work across emails, chats, and spreadsheets, everything is centralized in one place with clear ownership and status. For me, this improves productivity by making it easy to prioritize tasks, follow progress, and avoid missing deadlines. Features like boards, automation, and reporting reduce manual tracking and help the team stay aligned. It also bridges gaps between tools through integrations with platforms like GitHub and Slack, which keeps development updates and communication connected. Overall, it saves time, reduces confusion, and makes project execution more organized and efficient. Flexible and Easy to Use, but Can Get Complex

reviewText: It creates a consistent rhythm for how work moves across teams. It makes it easy to break down large features into manageable tasks, which helps during planning and execution. Improves visibility into progress, helping teams avoid surprises during releases. Ensures accountability by clearly defining ownership and status. Helps QA and engineering stay aligned through shared tracking of bugs and features. Supports better decision-making with clear historical data. reviewText: What I value most about Jira is its balance of robust integrations and AI-driven intelligence, which streamline complex cross-functional workflows. Additionally, performance lag in data-heavy boards and a complex pricing structure—where essential features often require paid add-ons—can diminish the overall ROI. I also appreciate how a task can be quantified using story points, details, and epics. Jira makes project management easy, especially with the breakdown of projects from epics to stories to subtasks, which really simplifies the workflow. Additionally, I like how Jira displays various formats like code, links, and charts from Miro, etc. AI suggestions can be better, and while I like how Jira displays various formats, it could be more graphically appealing. Jira makes project management easy by allowing projects to be broken down from epics to stories to subtasks, simplifying work. Transforms Project Management with AI and Integrations

title: Powerful, Structured Agile Tracking with Jira
reviewText: Jira is a highly structured tool for managing development tasks and tracking progress, and I use it regularly to handle user stories, bugs, and day-to-day tasks. Its ease of use improves over time. At first it can feel very detailed and a bit overwhelming, but once you get used to it, creating tickets, updating statuses, adding comments, and following progress becomes a consistent, systematic process. One of Jira’s strongest points is the breadth of features available. It supports sprint planning, backlog management, workflows, dashboards, and reporting, which makes it a good fit for Agile teams that need clear visibility into ongoing work. When it comes to integrations, Jira works very well with tools like Bitbucket and Confluence. Being able to link commits, pull requests, and documentation directly to tickets helps maintain clear traceability between development activity and the tasks being worked on. I use Jira every day throughout the development cycle to track tasks, update progress, and manage work items. From an implementation standpoint, the initial setup and configuration can be fairly complex. Setting up workflows, permissions, and project structures takes upfront planning and, in some cases, help from an admin. The interface can also feel a bit heavy, particularly when you’re switching between multiple boards, filters, or reports. When it comes to customer support, the documentation and community resources are solid, but the experience with direct support can vary depending on the plan. For smaller teams, Jira can sometimes feel too detailed if you only need straightforward task tracking. Jira brings structure to project management by organizing tasks, tracking progress, and helping maintain accountability across teams. It improves visibility into who is working on what, makes it easier to follow bug resolution, and supports sprint-based planning. Overall, it reduces confusion and helps ensure development work follows a clear, trackable process. Powerful, Structured Agile Tracking with Jira

title: The Ultimate Engine for Scalable Workflow Efficiency
reviewText: Jira’s greatest strength is its unmatched flexibility, allowing us to build highly customized workflows and automated processes that scale perfectly with our team's evolving needs. While Jira is incredibly powerful, its extensive feature set can lead to a steep learning curve and a cluttered interface that occasionally makes simple navigation feel overly complex. Jira solves the problem of fragmented communication and manual task tracking by centralizing workflows into a single, automated source of truth, which allows our team to stay aligned and increase our delivery speed. The Ultimate Engine for Scalable Workflow Efficiency

title: Flexible, All-in-One Tracking with Strong Integrations and Insightful Dashboards
reviewText: Very flexible to use. You can set it up to match how your team works—whether that’s Scrum, Kanban, or something custom. It’s also not just for developers; QA, product, and even non-technical teams can use it without much friction. Tracks everything in one place. You can follow a task from start to finish—requirement → development → testing → release—which makes coordination a lot easier, especially on larger teams. Works well with other tools. It integrates smoothly with tools like Git, CI/CD pipelines, and collaboration apps, so everything stays connected and up to date. Useful reports and dashboards. Features like burndown charts, sprint reports, and dashboards help teams stay on track and make better decisions. Great for managing both the small and large projects. Overall, it’s a strong tool when you need clear processes and reliable tracking at scale. AI features : Integration with browserstack enables easy test case creation and execution using AI Wast suport from the Jeera team. With large projects or lots of data, it can become slow or laggy. Navigating between boards, filters, and issues isn’t always smooth, especially for non-technical users. Setting up workflows, permissions, and fields can take time and effort. It’s easy to overcomplicate things if not managed well. Managing the project very smoothly. Dashboard and Sprint reports are great tools Flexible, All-in-One Tracking with Strong Integrations and Insightful Dashboards

title: Good for Tracking Progress and Staying on Schedule
reviewText: I like Jira because it keeps work organized and easy to follow. The UI is clean once you get used to it, and it’s simple to track tasks and progress. It also connects well with other tools we use, which makes things more convenient. In terms of performance, it’s generally reliable and handles projects well, even with bigger teams. Pricing can feel a bit high depending on the plan, but it’s worth it for the value you get, especially if your team uses it daily. The onboarding can take some time at first, but there’s good support and plenty of resources to help you figure things out. I also like the automation and AI features—they help save time on repetitive tasks and give useful insights. Overall, it’s a solid tool for managing projects and keeping everyone on the same page. One thing I don’t like about Jira is that it can feel a bit complicated at first. The setup and navigation aren’t always very intuitive, especially for new users. Sometimes it feels like there are too many options for simple tasks. It can also be a bit slow at times, and the pricing can add up depending on the features you need. Overall, it’s a powerful tool, but there’s definitely a learning curve. Jira helps solve the problem of keeping track of tasks and making sure nothing gets missed. Before using it, it was easy to lose track of who’s doing what or what the status of a task is. Now everything is in one place, so it’s easier to plan work, track progress, and meet deadlines. It also improves communication in the team because everyone can see updates in real time. Overall, it helps me stay organized and makes work more efficient. Good for Tracking Progress and Staying on Schedule

title: End-to-End Delivery Visibility with Custom Workflows and Seamless GitHub/Confluence Integration
reviewText: Jira has provided our development team with full visibility of all the steps involved in the delivery of the software, including backlog grooming, to deployment, due to its sophisticated issue tracking and workflows that can be customized. The sprint planning and roadmap are also especially useful, with the ability to establish achievable timelines and monitor our progress against the objectives without having to use both tools. The close connectivity with the GitHub and Confluence has been a game changer and our code, documentation, and project management have been linked to each other in a single seamless ecosystem. The interface of Jira may seem crowded, particularly to new team members, who take a lot of time to be onboarded and learn to navigate and use Jira safely. Initial setup also can be overwhelming, as the number of configuration options and settings can be overwhelming, sometimes needing a dedicated administrator to handle permissions, workflows, and project structures efficiently. The complexity in dealing with the software development process has been resolved with Jira providing our team with a well-organized framework to monitor the bugs, features, and sprints within a single framework. This has enhanced our accuracy in release planning and accountability at the team level, which has led to shorter delivery cycles and tasks that are not dropped in the cracks. End-to-End Delivery Visibility with Custom Workflows and Seamless GitHub/Confluence Integration

title: Asana is a great project management tool, especially when working with teams.
reviewText: Recurring tasks and simple task delegation make it easy to manage weekly tasks/projects and stay up to date on who is accomplishing each task and whether it has been completed. I also love Asana's 'boards' project type. It is a "Trello-like" experience and is great for managing progress on different projects when columns are designated for each step. I find that the "My Tasks" view often requires too much management of its own and can quickly get out of hand when it's not managed. This is a big reason why I say that Asana is great a project management but not the best at managing one individual's tasks/to-do list. The "My Tasks" view feels a bit unnatural and awkward and simply requires too much management of its own. Due date notifications are not ideal and Asana has set up their application in a way that requires you to "live" in the app in order to stay up to date with your tasks. Asana's mobile app has been one of it's weakest aspects. However, they have recently released new updates which has gone a long way to improve its usefulness. With that being said, it's still quite underpowered compared to its desktop web application. Asana is great as a project management solution, especially when working with teams. However, it isn't great at being a task manager/to-do list application for managing your own individual tasks. Asana has given me a streamlined method for managing projects and delegating tasks to other individuals. Viewing the status of a task that has been delegated is simple and I also receive notifications when a task is completed. Asana is a great project management tool, especially when working with teams.

title: Spectacular tool for project management with robust features and communication functionality
reviewText: Hi, my name is Lou Lou. I am a senior analyst in the CPG industry, and I give Asana an eight out of 10. We use Asana to manage our different projects that we have. Working across multiple cross-functional teams, it can sometimes be challenging to manage deliverables, set timelines, and hold everyone accountable, and Asana allows us to do that with multiple different functionalities. What I like best about Asana is the kanban board tool. This is a simple project management tool that is frequently used to manage different deliverables, and I have used that to allow our teams to see different steps of the process of what exactly we need to accomplish and ensure that the process flow is managed in a seamless manner. Something that could be improved about Asana is the tagging ability within instant messaging. A lot of companies use different instant messaging tools, however, the beauty of Asana is that you can actually communicate with your team within the software. However, tagging people can be a little bit problematic, and there have been bugs with the system in some occasions. If you're considering Asana, be sure to consider the training aspect of bringing your team up-to-speed on how to use the tool. Asana is a robust software. It has many different functionalities, and the learning curve can be a little bit steep. This isn't to say that it's challenging to implement. There are certainly functionalities that you can use on day one. But ensuring the whole team is bought in to the software is paramount to its success and ensuring that your organization is on the same page with using this amazing software.

title: Cool tool if anyone would actually adopt it
reviewText: I absolutely love the user interface - it's pretty seamless across mobile, tablet and desktop. It's pretty intuitive to the average non PMP-certified person. Drag and drop allows me to easily merge or separate personal and work tasks. Of course, it's fast and free so that always helps. Very difficult to integrate it into people's daily routines. Most people used their email or Outlook calendar to track their tasks and unless everyone is committed to using it, including the boss, the processes around the tool really fall apart, rendering usage of the tool sporadic at best. I'd recommend that anyone implementing consider how to use Asana in lieu of email and status in many cases. Otherwise, you run the risk of creating duplicative work and making your team hate it (and you)! Helped give me visibility into my team's to-do lists, upcoming deadlines, and assignments. It provided me with a paper trail on workflows and responsibilities. Cool tool if anyone would actually adopt it

title: Good for personal use, but lacking in enterprise level management
reviewText: I like the idea of workspaces and the ability to separate my personal tasks from my job tasks. Also I like the idea of create projects with tasks and subtasks. From an enterprise level standpoint, it lacks transparency. There's not really an easy quick way to show people priority or where a particular project sits in your queue. for personal use it's great. get it. If you are looking for an enterprise solution, go with AtTask. Good for personal use, but lacking in enterprise level management

title: Simple, Elegant Way to Capture and Share Tasks
reviewText: We used Azana for our last project implementing a web application for a financial services company. The best part about this application is there is very little magic to the whole thing. There are projects, tasks, and not much else. Entering a task is incredibly simple. You can easily share with other members your group. You can also reference other tasks anywhere in the application. Azana sort of forces a task management paradigm on your company. There are some situations where you can't quite fit what you are doing into the mold they provide. One good example of this if you have some sort of high level task that you would like a team to take care of, you must figure out a specific individual to delegate this task to. Some organization structures or teams of consultants do not have a project lead, therefore there is some guess work involved. Hopefully, in the near future they provide the ability to delegate tasks to teams and allow individuals to divide tasks or the manager of that group to delegate as he/she sees fit. Asana is a fairly solid project management tool. There are other very good solutions on the market at this point in time, also consider using Basecamp, Trello, Pivotal Tracker (software developers). If you do decide to use Asana, spend some time upfront to think about how you would logically break down projects and tasks, so in the long term your lists are relatively short and easy to manage. The issue we are trying to solve is having our data and the things we are working on stored in multiple different places. The benefit to us is that we are more organized and aligned as a company. The issue tends to be that enforcing the workflow on our employees is sometimes very difficult, because not every wants to use this tool or finds that it makes us more organized. Simple, Elegant Way to Capture and Share Tasks

title: Great for team collaboration on tasks
reviewText: The tool is easy to use and has many use cases that can be easily supported as bug tracking, applicants tracking, implementation projects and more. We also used it to collect rejects from testing done by users during user acceptance testing sessions. Its good for both a self-task list or team working on a project together. Lot's of features for following tasks updates, posting a task by email. Even though the mobile app was very much improved lately, its still missing some important features (moving tasks between "Today", "Upcoming" and "Later".) you need to plan how to use it across workspaces and projects and how to effectively share with your team members. Great for team collaboration on tasks

title: Simple Group Project and Task Tracker
reviewText: Very easy to enter tasks, projects and share them among team members. The interface is easy to understand. It does everything that I want. Occasionally, I get lost between my personal tasks and the tasks that are assigned to me as a part of the business group that I'm a part of. For this software to be helpful, it needs to become a part of your daily routine. You'll realize increased value if you can convince other people you work with to use it, too. Simple Group Project and Task Tracker

title: Best Work Management Tool in Market Right Now
reviewText: - It is easy to understand and use. - The UX and UI design are perfectly aligned with its purposes. - You don't need previous experience with related software to become proficient at this app. - It is the perfect tool to use daily to achieve your company goals. - It can be easily integrated into your team workflow. - Anyone without knowledge about Management Tools, can implement Asana into their company. - They have daily live events to train you for free as part of their customer support services. - The advanced plane is quite expensive for me. The organization inside my team. Everything was so chaotic before implementing Asana into our company. Right now it is helping us to be more efficient and deliver quality work in less time than usual. Best Work Management Tool in Market Right Now

title: Warning: Avoid This Company at All Costs
reviewText: Apparently if you aren't tricked into putting in your CC info, their free tier is solid. I signed up for what I thought was a free trial. Turns out, they required my credit card just to try the platform. I never used the service once, completely forgot about it (the trial was long), and then—without any notice—a charge of $60.98 hits my card. Let’s set aside how absurd that price is for a basic collaboration tool. I contacted support, explained that I never used the platform and didn’t even recall entering my credit card. Instead of owning the situation, they shuffled me between three different agents, each one more unhelpful than the last. The end result? No refund. Just a link to their “subscription terms.” This is a company that’s happy to take your money even if you never used their product. Zero integrity. Zero customer care. You've been warned. N/A Warning: Avoid This Company at All Costs

title: Poor experience with over billing - GO month to month if you use Asana vrs. being locked in.
reviewText: Depth of outside independent support available. Also, hiring new staff has used Asana more than most other project management software in the past. They offer a month-to-month pay option, so you don't have to get locked in when you decide to move to a different service. In month four billing, Asana auto-added a user to our account and billed us for them. Since they added someone who has not worked here in over a year, we ask them to remove this and issue a credit since it is clearly an error. Asana refused and cited their lengthy legal user agreement vs. Doing right by the customer and just issuing that single credit. Unfortunately, we wasted about three months with their product. It was allowing group colabration on projects and a quick view of the status of a project. Poor experience with over billing - GO month to month if you use Asana vrs. being locked in.

title: Best tool for a remote company
reviewText: The best thing about this software is not one, or two. I can create tasks, set their dates, routines, collaborate with my teammates, see their tasks, private my tasks, and so much more. It has AI features as well. It keeps you productive with its reporting feature. Its very easy to use. I literally learnt it within 2 days. Our team use this app daily. We have integrated it with slack and i can create tasks right from Slack. Its expensive. The pricing plan of dollars per seat make it expensive for a startup or a small organisation. Also, the feature are limited in the free version. We have a remote team of 10-15 members. We use Asana for our tasks management, content calender, project management and so much more. Its literally our most useful app right now. Everyone in our team uses asana daily. Its solving the problem of tasks management, and project management. Best tool for a remote company

title: Asana for Project Management and Workflow Automation
reviewText: I love how customizable it is. Almost everyone at my organization uses Asana completely different than other people. I love the flexibility of being able to organization and plan projects in a way that makes sense for you. Personally, I love all of the integrations and workflow automations that can be established. I was able to build out a custom Applicant Tracking System following a webinar posted on Asana's help website. It made a huge difference when bringing our hiring in-house this year. We didn't have to pay for a separate ATS, super helpful. Sometimes the mac application crashes so I tend to use the website more often to prevent this from happening. Also as a administrator on the account, it's very hard to update Billing Owner/Admin access. We've had some turnover in our leadership and the Admin console is fairly hard to navigate when offboarding a user with admin/billing access. I sometimes feel like I have to jump through hoops or use back-doors to get into our admin platform. It shouldn't be that complicated. I shouldn't need to reach out to customer support to help manage a platform we pay for. We use Asana as a custom ATS (applicant tracking system) which saves our small organization a lot of money! Asana for Project Management and Workflow Automation

title: Easy to start, Advanced features to discover
reviewText: I started using Asana a year ago, and enjoy the ease of sharing information with my team in a somewhat organized way (somewhat because the team didn't develop protocols for consistent use at the beginning - we are disorganized, not asana!) I am using it more regularly now and enjoying the advanced features of custom fields and workflow. I LOVE the forms builder's integration <3 Great training system too. The only downside right now for our team is the cost (we are a small nonprofit) and the fact that we have 15 seats and 17 staff members. :D It's centralizing communications around our projects and keeping those discussions that in the past have flowed over email, text, Teams and phone, in one place. Using a tool like Asana makes it easier to surface (or in some cases impose) the project structure, to break things down into tasks and subtasks and improve accountability and the feeling of accomplishent. Easy to start, Advanced features to discover

title: La herramienta clave para optimizar la gestión de proyectos en equipo
reviewText: User-friendly interface: Asana's interface is user-friendly and intuitive, which makes it easy to navigate the platform and perform specific tasks. Wide range of functionalities: you can create and assign tasks, monitor task progress, collaborate in teams, and communicate with team members among other things. This makes it an extremely versatile and useful tool for a variety of project types. What I find most difficult is being able to paste text into tasks, as it erases all the formatting and the text looks ugly, which complicates things for me when I have tasks to copy and paste. Personally, the way to delete tasks seems a bit tedious to me, since there are two buttons and it's not very intuitive. It helps us at work to maintain relationships among all teams. Integrations are useful: Collaboration and team communication are further improved by integrating with a variety of other tools and platforms, such as Slack and Google Drive. La herramienta clave para optimizar la gestión de proyectos en equipo

title: User Friendly Project Management Tool!
reviewText: Asana makes it easy as a freelancer to work with clients and streamline project management with an intuitive and easy-to-use platform. It allowed me to cut down on searching through long email strings with clients, and manage everything directly within Asana, increasing productivity and communication. The best feature is that it allows creation of sub-tasks to break down larger projects, as well as providing a lot of flexibility with more views than other similar tools, such as board, list, calendar, workflow, and timeline views. It can often feel a bit overwhelming at times, and like it doesn't simplify project management, but actually makes it more complicated than it needs to be. I'd also like more flexible pricing options for freelancers and small businesses, as the premium plan can be a bit steep when compared to other software. Streamlines project management with clients while eliminating the need to send emails back and forwards. It also breaks down large-scale projects into smaller tasks and makes it easy to keep track of the status of each task. It has significantly improved communication with clients, and also helped to become more productive. User Friendly Project Management Tool!

title: Organized & User Friendly
reviewText: I have the ability to rank what is most important vs least important, and I love that I can create subtasks for each task. It's like having a task to do the laundry, but knowing you'll have to: Subtask 1 - fold the laundry, and Subtask 2 - put the laundry away. It's nice to be able to lay out specific sub tasks! I wish I was able to assign a task to more than one person for projects I'm working on with someone else. One person having all of the credit for a completed task is a little biased, and all the responsibility falls to the assignee rather than who is both equally responsible for it. I used to have papers and sticky notes everywhere to remind myself of small (and huge) tasks. With the help of Asana, I am able to keep everything in one place with due dates! I love that I can assign due dates and times! Organized & User Friendly

title: Efficient Task Management Made Easy with Asana
reviewText: I like how it can efficiently manage tasks and projects in a team setting. Its user-friendly interface and customizable options make it the best choice for individuals and organizations looking to streamline their workflow. Additionally, its integrations with other tools and its mobile app make it accessible and convenient for users on the go. Its numerous features are overwhelming, and it may take time to utilize its capabilities thoroughly. It also has limited integration options compared to other project management tools. It helps me organize tasks and projects into lists, boards, and calendars for clear visibility and prioritization; assign tasks and deadlines to team members, enabling teamwork and collaboration; Track progress and receive updates in real-time, keeping everyone informed and on the same page; Store and share files, attachments, and information related to tasks and projects in one place. Efficient Task Management Made Easy with Asana

title: Get Yourself Organized With Asana
reviewText: Asana offers a variety of views of what you put into it, and it provides several ways to tag or otherwise group tasks. There is a coherent logic that makes creating new tasks easy. Asana, why did you take away the ability to copy a subtask and move it to another task? Losing this ability adds time to tasks if they have to be duplicated, but only partly. Archiving also could be considerably better organized—so much so that I have taken to creating a separate (nonarchived) archive project and adding finished tasks there. This makes them easier to retrieve if necessary. Why isn't that a feature? This team (content) has multiple moving parts. We work with internal stakeholders, external customers, and cross-functional teams. Asana makes it much easier to keep track of all of these subtasks with a clear, easy-to-follow workflow. Get Yourself Organized With Asana

title: Flexible Project Management Tool
reviewText: AI enhancements for Project updates and a summary of actions are incredibly useful. Chat/comment function is still short of Slack, Basecamp, but improving. Asana manages projects similar to tools like Basecamp and Monday. It organizes teams and projects and allows full collaboration within the app without needing to go external third-party apps like Google Tasks and Chat. Those integrations do exist and there are hundreds of others. But they are not necessary, Flexible Project Management Tool

title: Project management software with just the right amount of gamification!
reviewText: The interface is beautiful and the gamified tweaks, like celebrations after completing tasks, make Asana fun to use. A fun user experience is the single most important factor in long-term use, in my opinion. I also love that Asana constantly solicits user feedback via platforms like the Asana Forum. Some of the integrations for essential functions need work. For example, integrations to Google Calendar are one-way, and Slack integrations either mean EVERY single change to a project is posted to the connected channel, or nothing is. There are also some features, like Goals and Reporting, which could be expanded. However, the bottom line for me is that Asana rolls out updates frequently; I know that fixes are coming, even if they don't make it into the next update. At my company, much of the "work about work" that goes into key job functions is not identified or well-known outside of the person responsible for completing the task. I use the Portfolios, Reporting, and Goals tools to quantify how much of my time is spent on data entry (work about work) vs. core responsibilities for my manager. Asana has helped me build a business case for growing our team and creating a formal process for allocating resources (like time and money) to projects by presenting the data in a manner that is compelling to my leadership team. Project management software with just the right amount of gamification!

title: Asana Offers Easy to Onboard Project Management for Remote Teams
reviewText: Asana is easy to use and easy to log into for people working in different locations. All members of the team can update information in real time and respond to each other. It is helpful to toggle between boards and calendar views to be able to see projects in process for an entire block of time allowing for accountability and planning. In some of the fields, such as calendar view, it is not always easy to edit a project, so you do have to remember to toggle between the different view options for full functionality. When working during team meetings, you also want to make sure everyone on the team is looking at the same information and the same view on the screen to track effectively. It does take some time to set up and onboard. Asana allows members of our team around the world to see projects that we are working on together in the form of both boards and a calendar view. This allows us to plan workflow and to also look for places where we can work together more effectively for project planning and management. The graphic interface of Asana is easy to use and allows everyone to see things in real time. Asana Offers Easy to Onboard Project Management for Remote Teams

title: Manage your work & personal life
reviewText: Asana continues to release new features to further expand its product and makes improvements as necessary. What I've appreciated the most about Asana is that it has a very active community in the Asana Community Forum with so many great people that help find solutions to common problems and more. The way teams and projects are laid out could be expanded in my opinion. It would be great to be able to go an additional layer and have the ability to create sub-teams, sub-projects, and sub-sections. Asana also can't let end-users with the same company domain act as "guests." This has limitations as it then requires everyone that needs to contribute to a project to have a license which can be expensive. Asana has made tracking project work more visible and transparent. Through the use of rules, status updates, and workflows, I've been able to automate some of my work which gives me time back and allows me to focus on other priorities. Manage your work & personal life

title: The most intuitive list-making and time management tool I've found so far
reviewText: My favorite feature of Asana is the simplest: the fact that I can add any tasks to my to-do list along with all the information I collect along the way. It also allows me to track what I have done, which I am terrible at doing myself, so it can be a great asset for professionals who want to build esteem in their craft and also be able to measure the outcomes of their contributions. It is unhelpful that you can only assign a task to one person. Other people can be included on a task as "collaborators" by using the person's @name in the comments, but that is one of the things I noticed when I wanted to have both people review a document without having to create a task for each person. I work in marketing, so there are often ad-hoc and project-related requests that I need to keep track of, complete, follow up on, or a combination of those. Asana allows me to group things together in terms of where they fall in priority, time constraints, and a series of other workflows to help me work efficiently as a department of 1. The most intuitive list-making and time management tool I've found so far

title: Great cross functional, cross team tool
reviewText: The ease of creating tasks that are both related to projects, but also tasks that are 100% individual. The ease of collaboration on tasks is fantastic and being able to set date ranges for the due dates helps with giving the member enough heads up about when tasks are required to be completed Half of our team (the development team) live in Jira. One issue is that our development team doesn't live in Asana as the marketing and design teams do. This can cause issues the development team need to make conscious decisions to be checking Asana to keep up to date. A 2 way, easy integration between the two would be amazing. Make sure that all your team are using this and are all in on it. It's either all or none The problem that Asana is helping us solve is ensuring that all departments are up to date and up to speed with what is required for each project. From development to testing, and then to marketing. Great cross functional, cross team tool

title: Great Experience using Asana for Project Management
reviewText: You can add multiple projects at the same time. Improve mobile app. Make it more iser friendly. Please use it for project management it will make your life easier. I am doing freelance digital marketing projects and it helps me organize my clients and tasks. Great Experience using Asana for Project Management

title: Asana is great to keep myself organized, but it's sometimes cluttered/chaotic.
reviewText: I love being able to click on "My Tasks" to see what I need to prioritize based on deadlines, including projects from multiple workstreams. This makes it easy to stay on top of my work and ensure I am getting things done promptly. I don't like when I have random people in my organization assigning me tasks in Asana without any context or explanation... like I think there should be an option to either reject tasks or ask for additional information before they are automatically put into your task list. For this to be a good investment, you need to make sure that your *whole team* is invested/committed to learning & using Asana. I've found that when some people blow it off or ignore Asana, it makes the product less useful to the rest of the team because it doesn't contain the most current or up-to-date information. In this case, Asana becomes more of a time suck than a time saver. The main problem we are solving is keeping medium- and longer-term projects organized in a single location, particularly when they involve multiple people or multiple departments. Being able to lay out timelines is also really helpful in tracking progress. We've also found that Asana can be useful for things we may have previously done in an Excel spreadsheet, like creating our annual budget. Asana is great to keep myself organized, but it's sometimes cluttered/chaotic.

title: Simple yet effective task management platform.
reviewText: My favorite parts of utilizing Asana are the community, the ease of use, and their knowledge base. The community is quite active and can often problem solve or explain alternative methods for the vast majority of issues. Asana is a simple task management platform, that lacks the customizability of some of its more sophisticated counter-parts, but is easy to deploy and train on. Asana is missing some common sense functionality that would enable power users to get more out of the platform. Specifically, things like conditional logic for rules or the ability to more actively integrate the platform with outside software (the Asana API seems to struggle when dealing with massive workspace [1+ million tasks, 10,000+ projects]) and so that's another option that would be good for improvement. If you'd like an ease to use and quickly implemented task management software platform, use Asana. Asana has reduced the amount of meetings that we need to have and it has simplified my ability to produce project updates, meeting summaries, and weekly reviews. My favorite feature here is posting portfolio updates to make concise text around what is being done for specific groups of projets. Simple yet effective task management platform.

title: One of the best collaborative tools for WFH teams
reviewText: Being my first experience with project management tools, working with Asana was really so easy. You will require little or no help to understand the task distribution and the overall workflow. Managing our day-to-day task with Asana is quite simple. For the project managers, creating a new project and its subtasks is straightforward. Once the tasks are created they can be easily assigned to teams, just by names. Actually, there is no demerits or disadvantages to this application. The app works the best for all scenarios and project handling. Even if I compare it with EverNote I will prefer using Asana as it covers all the worthy features for project management. However, if I had to list one demerit then it would be its pricing. Once the pricing structure for paid Asana editions will become less, it will do wonders in the IT market. For new startup businesses, who have just entered into IT industry, Asana is the best tool for teams collaboration. The way it offers an option to create multiple projects and their subtasks is super. On top of that, you can set up priorities and deadlines for a specific task. Managers can easily keep an eye on the progress of the work whether it's pending or has been completed. Though I have not used the Premium and Business features, the Basic version will also do a great job. In short, I would highly recommend it for the work for home culture. I feel Asana is capable of handling everything, basic to complex projects. Task handling is very smooth and you don't need to refer to any tutorials for it. Yes, I have used a limited set of features but the free version is still better than the other tools. Talking about its visuals, the interface is neat and easy to understand. You can keep a track of all the tasks that have been assigned to you and you can change their status accordingly as working, done or completed. One of the best collaborative tools for WFH teams

title: Couldn't work without it
reviewText: Excellent for task-heavy teams like us with guests that need to see only specific tasks on specific projects. The webhooks that are available via Zapier, Integromat, and Automate.io lack some key data. This makes our workflows significantly complex. Asana offers some automation features in the app called Rules but they are quite basic and they don't cover our needs. Task management with great project management features. It helps stay organized are all times. Couldn't work without it

title: Asana Explanation  - Multi-collaboration Platform that keeping groups and an organization together
reviewText: Asana is a beneficial software to have, particularly when your staff has gone entirely remote recently. Asana is my favorite project management tool since it allows me to organize work and tasks over several teams or departments. Asana tracks who is accountable for every job and clearly defines the phases of an assignment and the deadlines for each step. Collaboration on several tasks is made possible by using just one platform, and it interacts with a lot of additional items to make it even more helpful. Products such as Discord, Hub, and Firefox are examples. Working with others is very simple, which is my second favorite feature in Asana. Asana was clearly designed to be used by groups of individuals. This could become stressful while working on a relatively simple application that does not require these capabilities. Even when I'm using it alone or for a short job, I tend to become overwhelmed by the number of options available, and I don't always take advantage of all of its features. I'd suggest asana if you're looking for an easy-to-use solution for tracking and collaborating on projects. This is the appropriate tool to use. With the help of Asana, my organization and staff have been able to address a variety of challenges. For the monitoring duties for the actions required to accomplish them, we have already been employing this software. It is beneficial to have everything organized to allow me to understand who's really focusing on what and then when it is ready. In addition, we all can make suggestions in a single location that may be referred to in the later if necessary. This feature is essential because my firm has transitioned to a remote environment for the time being. It allows all of our departments to keep in touch. They are constantly introducing new things and updating their software, which would be a real advantage. Asana Explanation - Multi-collaboration Platform that keeping groups and an organization together

title: Fantastic PM Solution for Teams of All Kinds!
reviewText: Asana manages to be both robust and flexible at the same time, offering myriad ways to organize projects and teams. 10/10 The learning curve for new user adoption can be a bit steep, though most pick it up quickly. Asana helps to unify projects across different teams and bring together the organization as a whole. Having transparency and access to other teams' work that may affect your own is invaluable. Fantastic PM Solution for Teams of All Kinds!

title: A great project management solution for teams
reviewText: If you've ever managed a team, you know that it's challenging to stay on top of everything — especially complicated projects. Asana makes it easy for teams to organize and manage projects, regardless of size or scope. I really like the way Asana provides prebuilt templates for different types of projects, which makes it easy to get started. I also appreciate that most of the key features are available for free, making it easy to use and test Asana fully over a few months before committing to using it long-term. Honestly, there isn't much I don't like about Asana. One of the main downsides had been that there was no desktop application, but Asana recently added one that works well. By using Asana, our team has been able to stay on top of various projects and ensure we aren't letting anything slip through the cracks. One of the main ways we use Asana is to manage all of our external emails and social posts for various campaigns. It's a great way to make sure everyone on our team knows what is going out and when. A great project management solution for teams

title: Create a better work approach
reviewText: I have used Asana for more than a year, without a doubt, I think it is the best software to organize, manage all your projects and tasks. Asana is turned into a work tool since it has allowed me to increase productivity, organization and the ability to manage all my tasks followed by all my projects. It is excellent since it works very easy, it has an excellent desktop version, which in my opinion is quite simple and the mobile application is very fun and dynamic, this software allows you to upload documents and photos, without a doubt Asana is excellent to establish objectives and achieve improved project planning. It also has a free feature, which is great to get started with this tool. Honestly, there is nothing that I dislike about Asana, it is a very easy and simple to use tool, it is excellent to be able to work with your entire team from a single place. Asana has proven to be an application that helps optimize productivity and organization with your projects and tasks. Asana has really proven to be one of the best software that helps to organize, schedule and maintain an excellent management of tasks and projects, it is ideal for small and large companies. I have been able to navigate through Asana and so far it has seemed very simple, I have been able to use the mobile application, manage calendars, emails and documents, and it has also allowed me to schedule tasks, make projections and track all my activities and status. It has excellent reporting and analysis, subtask creation, it also allows you to perform tasks for a higher level and import tasks. Create a better work approach

title: My Asana experience
reviewText: I always likes asana User interfaces becasue it makes it easy for us to be used from the beginning. I can easily set up the work and I request my fellow friends to participate and to do everything simultaneously. I also enjoy keeping count of the duties I've allocated, and they'll be reminded to accomplish the duties. I may utilize the working calendars to work out and reorganize the scheduled start times, durations and job ends to integrate documents, reports or e-mails. I wish it was simple to assign dates to reoccurring tasks. The possibility to ignore certain information would be fantastic. I recommend this software, a very good tool. When you don't develop the organizer, the task items might be lost. Because using Asana, I could continually be organized and talk to the clients every day, which helped me to keep working time consistently. also, asana offers a great system for alerts and reminders My Asana experience

title: Asana as a Project Management tool
reviewText: It has groups and categories to divide your different projects, and it allows you to collaborate with your team members on various projects. My boss knows what projects I am working on and what projects I have completed. I also like that I can customize my dashboard and my tasks. title: Reallocate your Project Management team
reviewText: Easy to coordinate across multiple teams, assign tasks, and use for our agile marketing practices. It allowed us to reassign our project managers to other in-demand marketing functions, while letting the software do the work for us in terms of keeping the team organized and on track. The software is not super intuitive; there is a significant learning curve. Difficult to onboard someone new to the platform - it takes a long time to get them up and running. Additionally, I wish Asana had more functionality to automate regularly cadenced workflows - like resurfacing quarterly/annual campaigns we run at previously noted timeframes. If Asana had these future-facing automated workflows, it would definitely be a 10/10 for me. Our team works out of multiple offices, so it helps a team that is not co-located work effectively together. Reallocate your Project Management team

title: Asana: a cycle of productivity and order from start to finish
reviewText: Asana has App for Android and IOS that allows us to be informed where we are in real time. Through the boards, Asana offers a full visualization of the phases of the general project, which allows you to always have “in view” the status of each of the activities to be carried out, in the process of development or officially completed. With the help of Asana's calendar features, you can get an overview of your responsibilities, commitments, and timely reminders of both your individual activities and those of the overall work team. Asana offers a series of functionalities that are worth knowing if you want to delve into the use of this tool. Asana is a web and mobile application that allows you to create, distribute and manage the workflow of tasks and projects in order to maintain a cycle of productivity and order from start to finish. The best way to attack large responsibilities is by dividing them into classified projects, therefore, this application allows to host workspaces dedicated to a specific project, within which it is possible to create tasks and subtasks separated by order of priority or execution, designating with clarity to those responsible for the work in each phase. If you are going to use Asana, it connects easily with other applications popularly used by remote work teams or freelancers who want to organize their online workspaces in an optimal way. For example, tools like the email you possibly use, Slack, Jira, Google Calendar, Google Drive, Dropbox, GitHub, Trello, Evernote, among many others, have the option to connect with Asana and merge purposes from a common space. Asana: a cycle of productivity and order from start to finish

With Asana you will have the opportunity to organize and plan projects through Gantt charts, and even the time in which the activities will be executed. Unlike other applications, this one offers a completely free version and very easy to use. That is, it allows you to carry out projects or tasks quickly and easily. title: Effective and Easy to Use
reviewText: Asana is an aesthetically pleasing and easy to use content management system. There was a bit of a learning curve because I've never used a CMS before, but all things considered, it was pretty simple to learn how to use Asana. I have already recommended Asana to other friends who needed some kind of a CMS in their own jobs. I honestly can't think of any downsides to using Asana--it works well and does what I need a CMS to do. My only note is that it might be nice to be able to sort tasks in different ways on the "My Tasks" page; "Today," "Upcoming," and "Later" don't always fit as labels when I'm trying to keep track of all of the different things I have to do. Asana makes organizing the production of content across campaigns and with other teams fairly easy, which has helped us keep things running smoothly. It's very helpful to always know exactly where something is in the pipeline and who touched it last. Our workflow has been streamlined and it gives us something to show our managers when they want to know what we've done and will do in the future. Effective and Easy to Use

title: Failure to Innovate or Listen to its Customer Base
reviewText: The aesthetic of the UI is nice and it is fairly responsive. Features requested by the community are ignored for the most part. There are dozens upon dozens of basic requests to make the software more functional that the team at Asana has ignored for years (4+ years in many cases). Here are some examples: • Lack of Markdown support. • Inability to add subtasks to the timeline view (making scheduling impossible). • Inability to add subtasks to the workload view (making resource planning impossible). • Inability to assign multiple people to a single task (Asana says we don't need this). • Inability to set different status levels for list vs board view. • Inability to make task templates (only project templates) • No built-in time tracking. • No transparent roadmap — the engineering/product team keeps silent. Unlike other competitors that are quite open with their dev cycle. • Cannot control who can/cannot delete tasks from projects. • Inability to reverse the order of comments (newest comments require a lot of scrolling to get to) • Inability to add custom fields to the "My tasks" view. • No desktop application. • Limited sorting/filtering options (no nested sorting for example). • Limited to one form per project. • Inability to mark sections complete (only tasks and subtasks). • Subtasks are not automatically added to the project (in your reports view it will show them as belonging to "No project"). • Inability to hide weekends from the timeline view. • Inability to drill down to the day level in the timeline view (inability to see hours in a day). • No support for calculated fields. Don't get sucked into it. It's hard to leave, but not because it's great. There aren't many other alternative out there that are any better though. Clickup is the one tool that is capable of doing basically everything you may want but they suffer from some speed issues currently. Review their Feedback Forum and pay attention to the requests that are open as well as how long they have been open...if you're okay with a team that ignores its user base for years on end then this may be the tool for you. None currently — we keep trying to use it but we spend more time working on making it work than we do getting work done. We really want it to work but it's just not flexible enough to be satisfying yet. We're still using it though — we're sort of stuck with it. Failure to Innovate or Listen to its Customer Base

title: Asana is excellent for small teams to coordinate without missing a beat.
reviewText: My team primarily uses the boards and timelines to view projects. These two views are really helpful for seeing your project at a glance. The timeline in particular is really helpful for me as a research coordinator, because I can see dependencies and what might be coming down the road that needs our attention. I also really love the Progress screen for really highlighting where we are with a project. I use this to celebrate what we've accomplished, give updates that may not be attached to a specific task, and throw up a flag when something is rapidly coming due. It limits surprises and forces us all onto the same page each week. The first problem that we encountered was getting the whole team onboard with using it. For Asana to work, it ALL has to be in there--no scribbled notes on a notepad and a one-off email. Everything has to come back so that the content is consistently updated. My team was more used to a pen/paper to-do list and email conversations for every task, so there's been an adjustment period. We've still had a lot of success, but has required a little extra effort on my part. We've also felt that some of the built-in options just aren't varied enough to capture our projects. For instance, while I love the Progress screen, it is frustrating that there is no way to indicate that a project is on-hold (a MUST for us during the pandemic!). There is a very robust online community and certification courses to help get your team rolling if you choose Asana. I took all the courses to become an asana ambassador because it gave me what I needed to really explain how to use it to the rest of my team. One benefit I didn't expect was how great it's been personally too. I have an account for work and then a linked account that is just my partner and I. We use it to plan meals, make our grocery lists, and we're even using it to keep organized as we're purchasing our first house! Asana is excellent for small teams to coordinate without missing a beat.

title: I have never felt so good working remotely before, Asana is the ideal tool for me.
reviewText: I have never had any conflict when using Asana, I have even uploaded tasks myself and added the team members who must carry it out, the gantt chart is one of its best features since it allows you to move the tasks together and not only individually It is particularly helpful in situations such as the current health contingency, it allowed us to move the tasks of all team members to advance or delay them, something that individually would have taken us around 5 days, we did it in 1 hour. I have tried all the functionalities and until now I have not had any conflict when using it, an excellent tool. A very good tool for working remotely, it has been a great help for my company now that we are working from home. When I work remotely I usually feel a bit of disagreement because it is not possible to know the level of progress of the projects until the delivery, many times there are conflicts because people in the teams understand different things or plan through the phone, with asana already We have the possibility of working on a written requirement through a task, it is possible to know who will be involved, when we have to deliver it and also maintain continuous communication. I have never felt so good working remotely before, Asana is the ideal tool for me.

title: Easy to use and stay organized
reviewText: I like how Asana is super easy to use. You can quickly create projects, sections, sub-tasks, and even sub-sub tasks. You can assign tasks out to individuals, add due dates, make the task dependent on other tasks being done first, and more. The animations for completing a task are also very satisfying. We love how it's very easy to submit requests as well. One person can fill out a form and then it will automatically create a task in Asana and assign it to the right individual. Sometimes it's hard to get to a sub-task (or a sub-sub task). I wish there was a better view for that, but otherwise, I am very pleased with it. I also wish there was a way to hide all of the task updates. For example, if you edit a description for a task, the bottom of the task will say "John Smith updated the description 2 hours ago." When you make a lot of changes and start adding it other projects and assigning it out, the list gets very long. I wish there was a way to show/hide the task history. It's not that big of a deal, but it is something that would be nice. One other thing to improve would be the forms. You can create your own custom form, but some of the options are limited. I would like it to be more robust. I haven't tried other project management software, but Asana has a free version you can try out. We are more organized and can collaborate across teams more easily. We can plan ahead and know which projects we will be working on during the quarter. We know who is accountable and where to get information about a project or task. Easy to use and stay organized

title: Favorite Task Management System for Myself and Our Team!
reviewText: My favorite part about Asana is the ability to go multiple levels deep for projects and tasks. I like that each subtask can be assigned with a due date. I also like how all of "My Tasks" are separated out so that I don't have to go into each individual project to figure out what I should be working on. Asana is great for collaborating on projects with others - I like how easy it is to drag & drop files into the tasks. Asana performs well on my cell phone and my iPad as well. I like that it updates cross-device so that I can always have the most up-to-date info and also add things on-the-go. I love the ability to forward emails into projects in Asana so that I don't lose track of things and can keep my inbox clear. I like that the free version of Asana is fully functional and offers great features (although there are very tempting things in the paid version)! I love the integration with Zapier so that we can use Google Forms to automate tasks coming from outside our team. I also like the ability to add people outside our organization (like a graphic designer) to collaborate on tasks with limited permissions. There have been a couple instances of Asana going down and I was not able to access the platform at all during that time. It is difficult to commit to using the platform wholly and then realize how totally reliant you are upon it once it's not available. I don't like that you have to upgrade all users in an organization to a paid subscription. I'd prefer to have the ability to choose on a user-level because we have some team members that use it much more than others and would benefit from the paid portion. But do not want to over-pay for the team members who don't take advantage of it. When considering Asana, I recommend fully committing to it to test it. It works best when your whole team is bought in. Our Marketing Team all uses it and cannot speak highly enough about it. We have helped other teams implement it in our organization as well and have found the best success when everyone is willing to fully commit for a couple weeks to really test it out. I recommend playing around with the platform to find what task/project structure works best for you and your workflow. It is different for everyone! We have had a few problems nailing down Zapier processes, but always able to figure it out. Sometimes the fields as they appear in Asana are not ideal for that connection, but we make it work! Our team has been able to drastically cut down on email communication back and forth, which has been incredibly helpful! Favorite Task Management System for Myself and Our Team!

title: Best project management and work tracking tool
reviewText: Project management is one of the key function for company to grow up. Asana is the best project management tool that I have ever seen. Normally it is free software, but If you want advanced features you can purchase payable packages, such as Premium, Business and Enterprise. Asana is very simple and no need any prior-knowledge. Its' user interface is very friendly with strong left-side menu. If anyone has the uncomfortable feeling about this project management tool, they included training section called "Asana Basics Training". Main functions of Asana are build or join a project, create tasks, allocate a task, add a follower to the task and deliver the task. Most valuable feature of Asana is workflow man management. By using it, we can create and tracking processes, configuration with calendar. This tool has well organized task flow from start to end, as a result of that we can make sure our project is on the right - way. Reporting and data representative method of Asana is very attractive. Calendar, timeline and Kanban boards are visualizing our project by providing well graphical interfaces. As a result of these things Asana became our best project management tool. As a free version they allow to 15 teammates only. And also sub task management is poor, But overall Asana is a good tool for project management. If you are doing projects with time frame, you must use Asana. It will ensure that your project will deliver on-date, As a multi-location company, our main problem is auditing sub locations' work. After the installation of Asana system, now we can monitor it from our main location. And also our project finished on-date. Now We can get individual performance of each employee by using Asana. Best project management and work tracking tool

title: A good tool for managing team projects
reviewText: Asana is simple to set up and use. We literally got our department up and running in a day. This included adding team members and getting them up to speed (some of whom were non-technical). Adding multiple team members to a project item was super helpful and everyone assigned to the task can track the progress on that task from one common thread. This helped reduce the duplication of tasks by different team members assigned to a common task. Asan can also be configured to have multiple layouts and tasks on one layout can be linked to a different one, allowing progress to be tracked in ways that make sense in different situations. This is a feature that I probably didn't explain well but I'm sure there are multiple video tutorials online that would do a better job of demonstrating this. I also enjoy the templating feature that helps us save time by starting new projects based on a previous one. How much time is saved you may ask? Try restarting a complex project and figuring out what has to be in place from scratch everytime. The Asana app for mobile phones also has a fairly intuitive user experience (more on that later) and most of our team members were able to get on board early. Well, there isn't much to dislike about Asana. The out-of-the-box product is set up to send lots of emails notifying users of almost every single item to which they are tagged. This quickly gets annoying and turning off the multiple notifications easily solves this. I do understand the need for such reminders to be sent out, but I would have preferred a less aggressive notification approach especially when onboarding new users. Apart from this pet peeve of mine, Asana has been great for our team. Make the most of YouTube tutorials, they are a goldmine for getting started smoothly. We are using Asana to coordinate teaching in an academic department at a university. We use it to assign Faculty their classes, inform them of any important dates and announcements, as well as in managing the teaching process and sharing experiences. This has helped us to keep the important parts of our workflow front and center. It has helped us realize the inefficiency of normal email. It has also helped the team to get communicate and coordinate better than before. A good tool for managing team projects
