title: Perfect Task Manager
reviewText: Any problem can be planned as much as possible in detail. You can make projects under joint tasks, divide them into sections. Also, each task can have its own tags (not only standard ones but any ones you want). Design and usability are indeed at a high level. Especially when compared with the old version of the service or some similar system. The opportunity to invite a person for general work on projects. For the invitation, it is sufficient to know only the email. For team members, you can assign different levels of access. A very convenient option for visualizing tasks by days. It is easy to find out what tasks are planned for the next week. You can transfer tasks between days simply by dragging and dropping them. The Android application contains all the basic functions. However, it's much more convenient to work from the desktop. There is everything you need: projects, tasks, subtasks, a system of reminders, a calendar, discussions of tasks, in addition, a nice design and an intuitive interface. A free version is enough for the job. It provides for up to 15 participants. There is a Premium version with a number of additional features. We use almost all the functions of this service. For me, this is the best system of joint coordination. People in my team know exactly what to do and when to do it. My clients know exactly what is happening with their projects. Moreover, I know how to plan my day. Communication is directly related to tasks and results - this is really important. Perfect Task Manager

title: Great Management Tool
reviewText: Asana has awesome customization capabilities to tailor it to different types of tasks. It seems rare these days, but I've been very pleased with every single update they've rolled out over the last few years. They listen closely to their users and take feedback well. With this, it's actually a little difficult to find areas I truly dislike. Asana seems to hit the mark for what they created this platform for. Any other types of shortfalls that I've seen are nitpicky or technically out of their scope of business. Pair this application with Harvest for time tracking. This is a great way to get an honest test on how much time these tasks and project are taking you and/or your team to complete. We are an agency that manages about 100 clients between 40 employees. Asana is vital to keep everything running the way it should be. We pair it with Harvest to keep a close eye on the hours we spend on projects & tasks to ensure we're hitting the hours the client is paying for and avoiding going over hours on projects and tasks. Great Management Tool

title: Excellent Tool for any size team
reviewText: Asana is the best tool I've used for task management, particularly when it comes to documentation. So often things are lost in emails, and here everything is recorded so it is easy to refer back to things. There aren't things I necessarily dislike, but I think there are improvements that could be made to take it to another level. Adding "assignee" would be a big one, as well as being able to assign a task to a team and not necessarily an individual. Train your team so everyone is using it in the same way. I think this goes for every technology though, but setting a standard so no one gets lost in the many subtasks or projects will help everyone adopt quickly and effectively. Task management and cross-functional team communication. We use this to better communicate not only within our teams, but to assign tasks outside of our team and communicate deadlines in one place. Excellent Tool for any size team

title: Email is a terrible way to manager your projects
reviewText: Asana is the email killer. It puts everything in context of a task or conversation, give complete transparency to who is doing what, but has privacy too, if needed. Do you ever get added to an email and it doesn't have the original attachment? Or how about the double email, opps forgot the attachment. How about an email about X and a subject line that says Y. No more with asana, context is king and managing a task or project in context kills it every time. There's not much to dislike about asana, and I'm being completely honest. Maybe the search could be better, but its 10x better than Outlook, so it's pretty hard to complain about it being a weakness. title: Asana: How I got hours of my day back
reviewText: My name is Danielle, and I am the Corporate Partnership and Events Manager for a nonprofit in the health care industry. And I give Asana an eight out of 10 rating. So with my nonprofit, we are scattered all across the United States, and we use a lot of collaboration tools to communicate with each other and to accomplish tasks. And with Asana, we're able to put all of that in one location to save time, confusion, and to ensure that all of our projects and campaigns are being accomplished to the best they can be. What I like best about Asana is the Outlook integration. It allows me to see everything in one place. And it also allows me to see what other people have due to me in my Outlook calendar. One thing I dislike about Asana is the lack of project control in some aspects. I am unable to move the dates of an entire project a week forward or a week back. Instead, I am having to go into each individual project or task and move that date individually. If you're going to use Asana, you should check out the commenting feature. It allows you to have an entire record of the task. And you should also check out the following feature. It allows you to attach as many people as you want to a task for a project so they can be fully updated on the status.

title: Excellent, Light-Weight PM Software
reviewText: Its UI and look and feel are great and intuitive and provides several options for visualizing projects and development. Team member controls are also great. Overall, it's a fantastic software product at a great price. It doesn't have super-detailed functionality that some other PM software's have, such sub-sub-tasks and multi-tiered task dependencies, etc., as well as a handful of other bells and whistles. That being said, most of that would be overkill for most small to mid-size businesses. Unless your business size or process require a high level of complexity for project management, Asana should give you what you need. Just give it a trial and feel it out. Consider leveraging some of its functionality in unconventional ways. It has made it possible for us to streamline all of our production processes and projects, greatly increasing our efficiency as an organization. Excellent, Light-Weight PM Software

title: Great for Task Management
reviewText: Asana keeps me on task with my own personal project items, and I can easily assign tasks to others involved in projects. I like that I can loop others in on one-off tasks and continue to update the progress knowing that they have access to see the updates as well. I also like the integrations with Google Calendar and Slack. Sub-tasks are clunky. I'd prefer the ability to expand/collapse sub tasks rather than having to move to another screen. For basic project management and task management this is pretty useful. You could integrate with Instagantt if you want Gantt charting capabilities. Asana also offers decent visual capability to see your task and project progress Organized project management across multiple teams. Great for Task Management

title: Good for Scrum, Sometimes
reviewText: Asana is really nice to track your progress, if you know how to properly turn big goals into projects of smaller goals and tasks. It's also fun when the unicorns come up because you've been doing a good job.. It's also awesome that it tells you if you have multiple of the same tasks but elsewhere in a project. And the assignment of tasks with followers is helpful. The only issue with Asana is that it doesn't load very well sometimes. And sometimes the task requires more than one person but the task in Asana allows only one assignee. It would also be nice if there were more specific recurring tasks, similar to daily but only every weekday instead of everyday. I generally have Asana in my Chrome window always open, but when I close my laptop and re-open it, it takes hangs a bit while trying to reconnect to the server. It's really nice if you want a visual representation of how your entire company is working. (If you are working in a small company) It could work well for small teams so there's no miscommunication on who's working on what. But if you're willing to deal with the small bugs in Asana every now and then. It's definitely worth it. We use it every morning for scrum so that everyone knows what everyone else is doing. It also helps with it comes to planning meetings every Monday. Good for Scrum, Sometimes

title: Asana is as simple or complex of a solution you want it to be
reviewText: Their free plan is a great way to get in and poke your head around to see if it’s right for you. The task manager is great for people that are super-detailed as well as teams that need to collaborate on projects both big and small. Asana is setup to automate much of the communication around project management including when things have been completed, when they need to be finished, etc. If you are working on lots of visual content, Asana doesn’t help much. You will need to pair this solution with something like InVision. Lastly, their Android app is pretty crummy, but they promise they are working on an update. If you are looking at other solutions, I would check out Podio. They have a pretty unique offering. We selected Asana so that we can ease up on the number of emails being sent around projects and to make sure everyone is on the same page. It solves these problems and more. Asana is as simple or complex of a solution you want it to be

title: Always has the features I need. Intuitive for implementers.
reviewText: 1. Infinite depth to Tasks (Subtask of a Subtask of a Subtask of a Subtask) - and each iteration has comments, attachments, due dates (recurring or one-time), assignment to person, and description fields. 2. Workspace > Project > Tasks structure is brilliant. 3. Ability to assign stuff, tag stuff, and favorite (follow) stuff is easy and quick. 4. Way better than Trello for non-project manager types. I am not a project manager. But I do a LOT of work. I've had over 50 weekly recurring tasks set up on Asana - Trello is a nightmare with that volume. 1. Asana doesn't allow moving a Task from one Project to another. You have to rebuild it from scratch, which is a pain in the butt. 2. The color scheme could be better. That may seem small, but when you're looking at all these tasks and they're the same color tabs, it gets a little overwhelming after a bit. 3. Sorting could be simpler. It's possible to sort tasks in several ways, but not intuitive. Run a trial with Basecamp, and give Trello and Asana a shot. You'll figure out pretty quickly which one works for you and your team. 1. Organizing projects and tasks for optimal execution. 2. Notifying others of changes in tasks and assigning duties as needed. 3. Making sure nothing gets lost in the process. 4. Not being miserable while doing all of the above. Always has the features I need. Intuitive for implementers.

title: Asana - Connection gone wrong!
reviewText: Tried using it to track deadlines and the work done in the team. But did not work. Lack of smartphone sync... I mean, its there, but not at all good. It keeps track of what people are doing, meeting deadlines, or atleast it tries to. Accountability is not realized anyways. Asana - Connection gone wrong!

title: Simple and Intuitive
reviewText: Asana is a bare bones task management and team collaboration service—and that's a good thing. It is not convoluted or cluttered with unnecessary features or options. Asana makes it easy to create, assign, and collaborate with team members. They even have a great Chrome extension to make a quick task or turn the webpage your on in to an actionable item. In keeping the software simple, there are a few features like progress tracking that are not available. It would be nice to mark a task as started, scheduled, complete, reviewed, etc. This isn't, however, a deal breaker for me as it is still great software. If you need a good, simple, and elegant way to keep track of tasks and collaborate with team members, give Asana a try. We use Asana to collaborate projects amongst our team as well as to coordinate launch schedules. Simple and Intuitive

title: A very nice task (not project) management system
reviewText: I'd say my favorite things about Asana is its speed. Not only does most everything open or respond immediately, it also is enhanced with awesome keyboard shortcuts. The recent teams functionality was a huge step forward for them. Free for small teams! I don't like that inflexibility with who tasks are assigned to. Currently you can only assign a task to 1 person (http://asana.com/guide/tasks/assigning). They're work-around is to create subtasks and assign the broken up pieces to different people. I understand the methodology, but the lack of flexibility makes it very difficult to use for all of your projects. The priorities are not consistent. If you prioritize a project by listing your tasks in order of priority, it will not display in the same order when a team member views the same tasks in their view. The transparency of what's left to do is poor - the tasks all pile up in a huge list and you have no idea where to start. Finally, it lacks core project management functionality. It's really a slick task management system, not project management. Looking for a quick and simple place to list tasks - Asana may be for you. A very nice task (not project) management system

title: Team task -listing and checklists; simple, collaborative on the cloud
reviewText: It's really easy to generate tasks and then assign them. 'CC' (carbon-copy) type functionality with Followers, and it's also easy to email in tasks to the tasklist . It's better than email in that it's a shared tasklist/checklist with additional functionality! I'm not sure this is much better than a well-coordinated checklist/tasklist - I mean, you can do much of the same functionality by building it into a good Google Docs spreadsheet, and it isn't yet another platform that the team needs to adopt Check out Trello (Kanban/card-system) for a more visual approach. Otherwise, if you're looking for a 'better' tasklist, then this can very well suit the bill. Also, consider using the keystrokes for even faster use of Asana. Team task -listing and checklists; simple, collaborative on the cloud

We’re trying to get new members of our team to learn Asana through the e-learning courses so we can use it in an efficient, consistent way. It helps to show teammates that when they don’t complete a task, it can block the rest of the project from moving forward. We recently added dependencies to our tasks (blocked by/blocking), and it’s made it easier for people to understand how a project is supposed to flow—and why, at times, it doesn’t. title: Asana has transformed how we manage projects
reviewText: Asana has been very helpful for our team. We’ve been using it for the past three years, and it has made our work much easier overall. Collaboration between team members is smoother, and ticket management is also far more manageable. Asana integrates well with tools our teams already use, helping keep project updates, communication, and reporting connected in one place. This reduces context switching and improves overall efficiency. Performance has been reliable even when managing large projects with multiple stakeholders. Pages load quickly, updates are reflected in real time, and the platform remains responsive during day-to-day use. The value provided by Asana outweighs the cost. Improved visibility, automation, and project tracking have reduced manual coordination efforts and helped teams save time while delivering work more efficiently. Overall, I had a positive experience with Asana. The main area for improvement would be reducing the learning curve for new users, since some advanced features take time to understand and can feel overwhelming at first. However, once you get the hang of Asana, I think it makes life much easier for managing every task, project, ticket, and more. Asana helps solve challenges related to project visibility, task tracking, communication, and coordination across teams. It reduces time spent on manual updates, keeps work organized in one place, and helps identify risks or blockers early. The AI features further improve productivity by automating routine tasks, summarizing updates, and helping teams make faster, more informed decisions. This enables me to spend more time on high-value work and less time on administrative activities. Asana has transformed how we manage projects

title: Asana Keeps Teams Aligned with an Intuitive, Visual Workflow
reviewText: What I like best about Asana is how it makes it easy to organize and track work in a clear, visual way that keeps teams aligned without needing constant check-ins. Its UI is intuitive with multiple views like lists and boards, integrations with tools like Slack and Google Workspace are smooth, and performance is generally fast and reliable even with larger projects. It offers strong ROI for teams managing complex workflows since it reduces missed tasks and improves accountability, onboarding is straightforward with helpful templates and guidance, and its AI features add value by helping summarize tasks and streamline planning. What I dislike about Asana is that it can feel overwhelming at first because of the number of features and views, and some advanced capabilities are locked behind higher pricing tiers. While it’s powerful, it can also become cluttered if teams don’t maintain structure, and its AI features are still somewhat limited compared to more specialized tools. Asana solves the problem of scattered task tracking and unclear ownership by centralizing projects, deadlines, and responsibilities in one organized system. This helps reduce missed tasks, improves visibility across teams, and makes it easier to prioritize work without relying on constant emails or follow-ups. For me, it benefits day-to-day work by keeping everything structured in one place, making progress easy to track, and ensuring accountability across projects. Asana Keeps Teams Aligned with an Intuitive, Visual Workflow

title: Asana Makes Task Organization Effortless at Work and at Home
reviewText: Asana is an extremely user-friendly application that is perfect for organizing and breaking down tasks. I use Asana both in my personal and work life. At home, my partner and I easily make tasks for each other which holds us accountable and allows us space to provide all of the details without rely on info-dumping to each other and remembering. At work, I have many duties that repeat at regular intervals and involve multiple steps, so making use of the recurring and subtask features has saved me so much time, effort, and energy. I'm aware of many integrations, though I don't personally utilize them. There have been only a few times I've ever seen Asana be "down" and most of those were datacenter-related rather than on Asana's end. Purchasing at least the first tier is essential for access to custom fields and rules for automation. The price is well worth the amount of brainpower those will save you. Between Asana's official help articles and community forums, I have been able to accomplish much of what I'd hoped to and more. I haven't had the need or desire to explore the AI capabilities, but I'm aware they are there should I want to explore them. I wish more of the community-voted ideas would be implemented timely. Many of them seem relatively simple, like formatting in task titles (bold, underline, color, etc.). The focus on AI teammates is a bit off-putting as I find it unreliable and missing the point of collaborating with actual humans. I have dozens of tasks that I'm responsible for that repeat at regular intervals. Having a hub where I can reliably see the tasks, when they're due, and my instructions is a huge time and energy saver. Asana Makes Task Organization Effortless at Work and at Home

title: A solid project management tool that keeps teams on the same page
reviewText: Asana is a really good project management tool that helps teams stay on the same page. It makes it easy for project managers and team leaders to check progress in real time, and you can track your team's bandwidth and productivity without much effort. The collaboration features work well for both internal teams and clients, which keeps communication smooth and reduces back-and-forth across emails. Overall it brings clarity to who is doing what, by when, and how things are progressing. The pricing can feel a bit steep for smaller teams, and the number of features can be overwhelming when you first start using it. There is a learning curve before everyone on the team gets comfortable, and some advanced views and reporting options are locked behind higher tier plans. Notifications can also pile up quickly if not configured carefully. Asana helps solve the problem of scattered communication and unclear ownership of tasks. Before, it was hard to know what was being worked on, what was blocked, and who was responsible. With Asana, project managers and team leaders can see real-time progress, monitor team bandwidth, and rebalance workloads when needed. It also makes client and cross-team collaboration much easier because everyone has visibility into the same plan and updates, so projects move forward with fewer status meetings and follow-ups. title: Centralized Task Management That Improved Team Coordination
reviewText: Asana helps our IT and operations teams manage tasks, service requests, follow-ups, and project activities in a centralized platform. While Asana is easy to use for basic task management, some advanced features require additional setup and training. Notification settings can become overwhelming when working on large projects with many collaborators. Reporting options are useful but could offer more customization without requiring premium features. Before using Asana, project updates and task tracking were managed through emails, spreadsheets, and chat messages, making it difficult to monitor progress and ownership. Asana provides a centralized workspace where tasks, deadlines, comments, and project status are tracked in one place. This has improved accountability, reduced missed follow-ups, and helped our team complete projects more efficiently. Centralized Task Management That Improved Team Coordination

title: Reducing Manual Coordination Effort Through Better Workflow Organization
reviewText: What I liked most about Asana was the visibility it provided while handling multiple project coordination activities, documentation follow-ups, and pending tasks simultaneously. During engineering coordination and review activities, I used task dependencies, timelines, and status tracking to monitor pending approvals, BOM updates, drawing-review tasks, and follow-up discussions without depending on scattered emails or manual spreadsheet tracking. The board and timeline views were especially useful because they helped quickly identify delayed activities and workload priorities across ongoing tasks. I also regularly used reminders and automated status updates to reduce manual follow-up effort during busy project cycles. Another practical benefit was the integration with Microsoft Outlook and documentation workflows, which helped centralize discussions, deadlines, and task visibility in one place instead of switching between multiple systems continuously. From a UI/UX perspective, the layout remained clean even while handling multiple projects, and onboarding was straightforward because task creation, assignment, and workflow setup were easy to understand without heavy training. I also found the AI-assisted task suggestions and automation workflows useful for improving prioritization and reducing repetitive coordination activities. One challenge I noticed is that notification volume can become excessive when working across multiple active projects and teams simultaneously. I also felt that managing larger project structures with many subtasks and dependencies occasionally becomes cluttered, especially during frequent workflow changes.Certain advanced automation, reporting, and dashboard features are restricted to higher-tier plans, which may feel limiting for smaller teams. Before using Asana, project coordination, pending-task tracking, and follow up activities were mainly managed through emails, personal notes, and spreadsheets, which made it difficult to monitor priorities and deadlines consistently. For example, while handling BOM updates, drawing reviews, documentation coordination, and multiple engineering follow-ups simultaneously, tracking pending ownership and status updates required repeated manual communication. Asana solved this by centralizing tasks, deadlines, ownership tracking, reminders, and project visibility into one structured workflow. Instead of manually following up through separate chats and emails, I could directly monitor task progress, pending activities, and responsibility assignments from the dashboard. This reduced coordination delays, improved visibility across ongoing engineering activities, and helped prioritize workload more effectively during high-volume project cycles. From an ROI perspective, the reduction in manual follow-ups and better deadline tracking improved workflow efficiency and reduced the chances of missing critical review or documentation activities. Reducing Manual Coordination Effort Through Better Workflow Organization

title: Asana: claridad visual, IA útil y automatizaciones potentes para gestionar proyectos
reviewText: Perfect! Let's switch gears to Asana. Here you have a well-structured, concise response focused on what makes this tool stand out in project management: What do you like most about Asana? What I value most about Asana is its visual clarity and relentless focus on accountability. It's the ultimate tool for knowing exactly who is doing what and by when, without any friction. UI/UX and Project Views: Its interface is clean, intuitive, and very smooth. I love being able to instantly switch between List view, Kanban board, or Timeline (Gantt). The drag-and-drop feature to adjust task dependencies on the timeline is a visual marvel that saves hours of planning. Workload Management: It's one of its best features. It allows you to see in real-time each team member's capacity to balance tasks, avoiding burnout and optimizing resources in a very visual way. Asana Intelligence (AI): The AI features add a lot of value. It generates automatic summaries of project status, identifies bottlenecks before they occur, and drafts professional status updates in seconds based on the team's progress. Integrations and Automations: The custom rule generator ("If X happens, do Y") is incredibly powerful and very easy to set up. Additionally, it integrates seamlessly with tools like Slack, Microsoft Teams, and Gmail, allowing you to turn conversations into tasks with a click. Support and Onboarding: The learning curve is very low. Its help center, the "Asana Academy," and the celebration animations when completing tasks (like flying unicorns) make adoption by non-technical teams quick and even fun. Unexpected benefit: The entry "Forms." I set up a form to receive external work requests that automatically turn into structured tasks within Asana. This completely eliminated chaotic last-minute request emails. Although it is a standard in project management, its main disadvantages lie in the rigidity of its pricing structure and the limitations in managing complex texts or notes. Price and Payment Plans (Price / ROI): The pricing structure is very rigid. Advanced plans have a fairly high cost per user for medium-sized teams. Additionally, they force you to pay for seat blocks instead of paying exactly for the number of users you have, which unnecessarily increases the budget. Limited Documentation (UI/UX): Asana is excellent for managing tasks, but it is terrible for creating long documents or team wikis. The task description has a very basic rich text editor, which forces you to rely on external tools (like Google Docs or Notion) for in-depth documentation. Single Task Assignment: A task can only be assigned to one person. Although I understand that philosophies like Project Management seek a single responsible party, in real life many tasks are collaborative, and having to create identical subtasks for each team member generates duplication and visual noise. Performance: agile and fast Overwhelming Alerts and Notifications: If you don't configure notifications with a magnifying glass from day one, the Asana inbox and your email get flooded with alerts for every small change, comment, or like, making the system chaotic and easy to ignore. Unexpected Frustration: The free version (Basic) has become extremely limited over time, cutting key features like advanced boards or task dependencies, which forces small businesses to pay before they are ready. Here is the response focused on the real problems that Asana solves and its direct impact on day-to-day productivity: What problems does Asana solve and how does it benefit you? The main problem that Asana solves is the lack of visibility and operational chaos in project management. It prevents deliverables from being left in limbo. Eliminates endless status meetings: It resolves the need to constantly ask "How is your task going?" or "Who is doing this?" Benefit: The entire team has absolute clarity in real time. You enter the platform and see who is responsible for each thing and what the deadline is, which reduces unnecessary meetings by half. Avoids bottlenecks and missed deadlines: It solves the problem of a project being stalled because one person didn't know their task depended on another finishing first. Benefit: Thanks to task dependencies and the Timeline view, the workflow is predictable. If something is delayed, the system visually alerts you to adjust the plan before it's too late. Centralizes project communication: It ends the problem of having project comments scattered between Slack, emails, and WhatsApp voice notes. Benefit: All conversation, attachments, and comments occur within the task itself. Anyone who joins the project late can read the history and catch up in minutes without losing context. Prevents team burnout: It resolves the lack of visibility over people's actual workload, preventing some from having too much work and others too little. Benefit: The Workload Management feature allows tasks to be balanced fairly, improving team morale and ensuring projects progress sustainably. In summary: Asana resolves the lack of accountability and disorganization of teams. The direct benefit is that we move from "reacting to fires" to planning and executing with total calm and control. Asana: claridad visual, IA útil y automatizaciones potentes para gestionar proyectos

title: Clean, Scalable Project Management That Keeps Workflows Organized
reviewText: I’ve been using Asana recently, and I can see why so many teams rely on it. The interface is really clean, it’s easy to organise projects, and it feels far less chaotic than trying to manage everything through spreadsheets and messages. It’s a good way to keep tasks, deadlines, and workflows in one place without overcomplicating things. Overall, it’s a solid option if you’re building systems and trying to scale properly. Asana is solid overall, but a few things can become frustrating once you start using it at scale. For simpler workflows, it can feel a bit over-engineered, and I sometimes find myself clicking through multiple layers just to make basic task updates. Notifications can also get noisy fast if you don’t take the time to fine-tune the settings. Another challenge is that some of the most useful features come with a fairly steep learning curve, so teams don’t always end up using the tool to its full potential. It’s definitely powerful, but that power brings setup friction and ongoing management overhead that isn’t always ideal for fast-moving work. Asana is one of those tools that really helps solve the “everything is everywhere” problem. It takes work that’s scattered across emails, chats, and spreadsheets and brings it into a single, structured system where tasks have clear owners, deadlines, and visibility. It also closes a lot of the accountability gap within teams. You can quickly see who’s doing what, what’s stuck, and what’s moving forward, without spending the whole day chasing updates. On top of that, it helps standardise workflows. Once you’ve set up templates and processes, repeatable work becomes much easier to manage and scale, without having to rebuild everything from scratch each time. Clean, Scalable Project Management That Keeps Workflows Organized

title: Great for Organizing Projects, but Task Moving and Google Sheets Alerts Need Work
reviewText: It helps organize projects of all kinds, from marketing work to the admin side. The automation rules are useful because you don’t have to keep manually changing statuses; alternatively, when you do change a status, it can automatically move tasks to the right place. Overall, it feels like it has more potential than how we’re currently using it, and I’d like to explore more of what it can do. There are a few limitations. For example, it can be hard to move tasks or subtasks around on different boards. I usually have to play around with it to figure it out, and it often feels quicker to delete the item and re-enter it in the right spot than to spend time working out how to move it properly. I also don’t like that it keeps adding a new message to a task when a Google Sheet is integrated and someone comments on that sheet. Most of the time the comment isn’t relevant to the task, but it still pings me as if it is. Keeping all projects in one place with clear deadlines—and being able to see other people’s tasks that are relevant to you without having to ask where they’re at—is very beneficial for saving time. title: Streamlined Project Management and Client Coordination
reviewText: I really liked Asana's clean and easy-to-use interface, which made managing client projects much smoother. The features like task assignment, timelines, comments, and status updates facilitated smooth collaboration, especially when managing client projects. What stood out was how Asana allowed us to track progress easily and keep both internal teams and clients aligned without relying heavily on emails or meetings. Features like task assignment and timelines helped our team clearly understand who was responsible for each activity and when it needed to be completed, improving accountability and reducing delays during client projects. Comments and status updates were particularly useful because all discussions, progress updates, and blockers stayed attached to the relevant task, helping internal teams and clients quickly understand the current status of work without long email threads or repeated follow-ups. Also, creating projects, assigning tasks, and organizing workflows in Asana was straightforward, and most team members could start using it quickly with minimal training. Notification management is the one area where Asana could be improved. In large projects with many team members, the number of updates and alerts can sometimes feel overwhelming and make it easy to miss important items. Asana could improve notifications by adding better filtering and priority settings. In large projects, too many alerts can become distracting, so customizable notifications for deadlines, mentions, and critical updates would help users focus on what matters most. I use Asana for project management and client coordination. It solves communication and project tracking issues by improving task visibility, accountability, and reducing confusion. Its features like timelines, comments, and status updates keep internal and client teams aligned without unnecessary emails or meetings. Streamlined Project Management and Client Coordination

The project flexibility allows every department to organize work in a way that suits their process, while still maintaining visibility and accountability. One thing that could be improved is the reporting and dashboard flexibility, especially for more advanced operational tracking. Some automations and integrations can also be sensitive when permissions or connected accounts change, so clearer alerts and easier troubleshooting would be helpful. It would be helpful to have alerts when something stops working, especially if the issue is related to permissions, a disconnected account, or a broken connection with an external tool like Teams or Outlook. It makes working on ongoing projects more efficient Customizable and Integrates Seamlessly, with Helpful AI Summaries

title: Asana Keeps Our Team Organized and Accountable
reviewText: I’ve only just started using Asana, but I really like how it helps me stay organized and on top of my tasks. Being able to share with my team, connect in groups, and assign tasks to one another is very helpful, and it also helps keep everyone accountable. its very robust, so there is a longer learning curve on how to use the app accountability - assigning tasks is very helpful Asana Keeps Our Team Organized and Accountable

title: Asana Makes Multi-Team Project Management Simple and Flexible
reviewText: What I like best about Asana is how simple it makes project and task management, even when handling multiple teams and deadlines at the same time. The interface is clean, easy to understand, and doesn’t take much time for new users to get comfortable with. I especially like the flexibility of viewing work in different formats like lists, boards, timelines, and calendars depending on the project requirement. It helps keep everything organized and improves visibility across teams. Task assignments, due dates, comments, and notifications work smoothly, which reduces follow-up emails and improves collaboration. Another thing I appreciate is the integration support with tools like Slack, Google Workspace, and Microsoft Teams. It helps centralize work and saves time switching between applications. Overall, Asana has helped improve productivity, accountability, and project tracking in day-to-day operations. One thing I dislike about Asana is that some advanced features are locked behind higher-tier plans, which can become expensive for growing teams. At times, managing very large projects with too many tasks and notifications can also feel overwhelming, especially for new users. The mobile app is useful for quick updates, but it occasionally feels less smooth compared to the desktop version when handling detailed project management tasks. Additionally, customization for reporting and dashboards could be more flexible without requiring premium features. Asana helps solve problems related to task tracking, team coordination, and overall project visibility. Before using it, we managed work through a mix of emails, chats, and spreadsheets, which often led to missed deadlines, duplicated efforts, and confusion about who owned which tasks. With Asana, everything is organized in one place. It’s much easier to assign responsibilities, track progress, and keep an eye on deadlines in real time. Automated reminders and status updates also reduce the need for manual follow-ups and help improve accountability across teams. It has improved collaboration between different departments as well, since everyone can clearly see project priorities, active tasks, and dependencies. As a result, productivity has increased, workflows feel more streamlined, and projects are completed more efficiently and on time. Asana Makes Multi-Team Project Management Simple and Flexible

title: Highly Customizable, Enhances Task Visibility
reviewText: I like that Asana is very customizable, allowing users to set up as many fields as they want and easily view them as tickets come in. This flexibility is great for tracking status and outcomes effectively. It also provides good visibility on outstanding tasks, which is crucial for managing reps' queries on commissions through the ticket system. To set up the workflow properly in Asana, a user needs to have experience or go through trainings and documentations. It seems intuitive but you really need to have some knowledge. Asana provides a customizable form for reps to enter information, set up automation workflows, and track status and outcomes, offering visibility on outstanding tasks. Highly Customizable, Enhances Task Visibility

title: Effective Client Management but Needs Improvement with Recurring Tasks
reviewText: I like that Asana allows for subtasks under a task, which makes it easy to organize complex tasks. I also appreciate having the ability to create private tasks for myself, so I can manage personal to-dos alongside my work-related ones. When you have a subtask with a date, and then a task with the date and you check off the task before the subtask, if it's recurring, it creates a lot of issues. I've figured out that the main task just should not have a date and the subtasks should if it's recurring. Asana solves the problem of identifying who is taking what task and outlines necessary steps to complete it. Effective Client Management but Needs Improvement with Recurring Tasks

title: Asana Keeps Teams on Track, and AI Helps with the Heavy Lifting
reviewText: The more a whole team utilizes its features and stays committed to communicating in it, Asana grows in its usefulness and utility for keeping a team on track towards projects' completion. Sometimes it's unintuitive to take your ideas and get that detailed into Asana. If you're Type B, like me, this product won't make you Type A. But it still does a great job filling in, and its AI tool can even do some of the heavy lifting for details and task creation. It's creating cohesion and uniformity across our team. It gives us a central place to track progress and communicate with each other. Asana Keeps Teams on Track, and AI Helps with the Heavy Lifting

title: Elevates Marketing Project Management
reviewText: I like Asana for the multiple views of the same task, dependencies, custom fields, automation, reminders, and visibility features. It gives me an in-depth view and keeps me on track. I can always know where projects and tasks are at, identify bottlenecks, and have all the information for each project brought together. The initial setup was very easy and I had no issues. AI reporting summary is not accurate and needs to be manually updated, and there's no way to highlight an area in tasks, which would be helpful to call out areas that need clarification. I use Asana to keep teams aligned, manage task status, and bring project info together, helping me see bottlenecks and track everything. Elevates Marketing Project Management

title: Solid Project Management, Needs Smoother Feature Integration
reviewText: I find Asana pretty simple to use, especially the basic features which are easy to figure out. It's easy-ish to connect it to Google Calendar. I can keep track of tasks efficiently and notify other team members about their tasks to ensure accountability and that we don't miss any tasks. I feel like there are so many features and it's hard to actually know how to slowly integrate more and more of them for the best Asana experience. If there was a way to automate - set the end date and create a new project that has updated deadlines based on the new end date that would be great. I use Asana for communication across teams, assigning tasks with deadlines, and tracking competing priorities. It helps notify and assign tasks to the right people, ensuring accountability. Solid Project Management, Needs Smoother Feature Integration

title: Visually Pleasing but Not Significantly Better than Basics
reviewText: I use Asana for tracking my tasks. I appreciate that it helps keep everything organized and allows for categorizing tasks. I like seeing what the people in my team are doing. The look of the platform is nice; I like the colors and how bright and airy it looks. I also like that you can assign tasks to certain people. The initial setup was easy and not too hard. To me, I don't think it's all that much better than just a paper list or Google Calendar tasks or an email. Asana helps me organize everything and categorize tasks while seeing what my team is doing. Visually Pleasing but Not Significantly Better than Basics

title: Asana Keeps Our Work Organized and Stress-Free
reviewText: What I like most about Asana is how simple and organized it makes everything feel. It’s very easy to track tasks, set deadlines, and quickly see who’s responsible for what without things getting messy. I also like having different views—like lists, boards, and timelines—so I can choose what works best for the way I’m working at the time. It helps keep the whole team on the same page, and overall it makes managing work feel a lot less stressful. What I dislike about Asana is that it can feel a bit overwhelming at times, especially when you’re dealing with large projects or too many tasks. The notifications can also get excessive if you’re part of multiple projects, which makes it easy to miss what actually matters. And while it’s powerful, some of the advanced features take time to figure out, so there’s a bit of a learning curve in the beginning. Asana helps solve the problem of scattered communication and unclear task ownership. Before using it, it was easy for things to slip through the cracks, and people weren’t always sure who was responsible for what. With Asana, everything lives in one place—tasks, deadlines, and updates—which gives the whole team much better visibility. For me, it’s especially helpful for staying organized and prioritizing what needs to get done. I can quickly check upcoming deadlines, track progress, and cut down on constant back-and-forth messages. Overall, it saves time, reduces confusion, and helps me stay on top of my work in a more efficient way. Asana Keeps Our Work Organized and Stress-Free

title: Effortless Project Management with Template Customization
reviewText: I like how easy it is to build templates in Asana and then customize each one for our specific trips. It makes sure everyone follows the same checklist while allowing us to add or delete tasks as needed, so projects aren't cluttered with unnecessary tasks. Tagging coworkers in tasks and assigning responsibilities is a breeze, and setting due dates helps a lot in organizing. The reminders that come through are really helpful because they take away the mental load of keeping track of each trip's task list. Overall, Asana provides managers with visibility into project status, prevents us from forgetting key steps in trip planning, and aids collaboration with the business office on invoices and wire payments. The initial setup was also very easy. On my dashboard, the customized views don't work very well. The automations don't update often enough to make it particularly useful. Asana provides visibility into project status, helps avoid missing steps in trip planning, and facilitates collaboration on invoices. Customizable templates and task tagging streamline project management, while reminders reduce my mental load. Effortless Project Management with Template Customization

title: Email Reminders and Automation Supercharge Productivity
reviewText: I use Asana for project management and it helps me increase productivity while staying on track and organized with a large number of clients. Email Reminders and Automation Supercharge Productivity

Simple Way to Work with Complex Data and Build Dashboards

title: A Review on Asana
reviewText: The best about Asana is you can easily track your project progress The pricing of the upgrade plan is a bit high. I use Asana to track down progress of my club project A Review on Asana

title: Easy Task Tracking and Clear Team Visibility in Asana
reviewText: I like how Asana keeps projects organized without feeling overwhelming. The timeline and task dependency features make it easy to see what needs to happen next and where delays might impact the team. I also appreciate being able to assign priorities, add comments directly within tasks, and keep all updates in one place instead of scattered across emails or chats. The calendar and board views are both useful depending on the type of work I’m managing, and switching between them is seamless. Notifications are helpful without being excessive, which makes it easier to stay on top of deadlines. Performance has been reliable for our day-to-day use, and the pricing feels reasonable for the value it provides. We haven’t explored many integrations yet, but the Slack and Google Drive connections look promising for improving collaboration. Setup was straightforward, and most of the guidance I needed was available through their help resources. I also haven’t had much opportunity to use the AI tools yet, but I’m interested to see how they evolve. I haven’t really found anything to dislike so far, as it has delivered everything we expected it to. It helps us to keep track of work without things getting lost in chats or emails. It’s made it much easier to see who’s working on what, what’s pending, and what’s coming up next. Before using it, we used to follow up manually a lot more, but now everything’s in one place and way easier to manage. Easy Task Tracking and Clear Team Visibility in Asana

title: Bridging Gantt Charts and To-Do Lists with deep functionality
reviewText: Asana bridges the gap between tasks on Gantt charts and personal to-do lists. That by itself is good, and achieved more simply and intuitively than I have seen it in a lot of older solutions. There is also a lot of depth to the tool, so tailoring to an environment is well supported. As an example, you can set up a project template that can help with supporting/enforcing a PM framework, or just give you some support to do things like employee onboarding in a reliably consistent way. The main downside I can see is that in an environment with established practices and tools around project management, work assignment, resourcing, and productivity, it might be complicated to either replace all of that with Asana, or replace part, and integrate the rest. I am creating a project delivery framework for my division, and so far Asana is ticking all the boxes that I wanted a framework support solution to tick. Bridging Gantt Charts and To-Do Lists with deep functionality

title: Asana Keeps Our Team Organised with Easy Workflows

Onboarding was pretty straight forward and the online help answered all questions we had. there isn't really anything, Asana is perfect for what we are using it for. title: Seamless Project Coordination and Team Collaboration with Asana
reviewText: I find Asana easy to manage because it centralizes everything I need—task creation, project planning, team assignments, and clear due‑date tracking. The onboarding tutorial stood out because it walked me through real examples, helped me understand how to structure workflows, and made it simple to jump into my team’s ongoing projects without confusion. Features like customizable boards, automated reminders, and progress dashboards make day‑to‑day coordination smoother and keep everyone aligned. I’d strongly recommend Asana to friends or colleagues and confidently rate it a 10 out of 10. I haven’t found anything to dislike. It’s been a smooth and positive experience overall. Asana helps me stay fully organized because it brings every part of my workflow into one place—tasks, projects, assignments, due dates, and team communication. What makes it stand out is how easy it becomes to coordinate multiple projects at the same time. Without Asana, it’s much harder to keep track of who’s doing what, follow progress across different teams, or make sure deadlines don’t slip through the cracks. With features like task dependencies, project timelines, automated reminders, and clear ownership, collaboration becomes structured instead of chaotic. The built‑in tutorial also made onboarding simple, showing me exactly how to set up projects, assign responsibilities, and monitor progress in real time. Seamless Project Coordination and Team Collaboration with Asana

title: A Versatile Project & Task Tracker, Perfect for Small Business
reviewText: ASANA is a very useful tool that we use in our business to keep track of our client projects and outputs, team meetings, and other management areas of our business like IT and Quality Systems. Once setup to our custom needs, navigating the interface is easy and performance of the program is quick. Updates are frequent and support are helpful when things change and we are not sure how to resolve problems. There seems to be a whole lot of other features that we do not use to its full potential, like AI features and software integrations. It is a very thorough product. Pricing is also feasible for our small business of less than 10 employees. I think the biggest issue is when things change. Updates will occasionally change the way things work, and we will need to spend a small amount of time finding a solution, or a work-around, or contacting support. It is a central program where we can organise multiple elements of our business, from customer outputs, to team meetings, to internal quality system management. A Versatile Project & Task Tracker, Perfect for Small Business

title: Intuitive UI with Great Collaboration, Needs Improved Customization
reviewText: I like that Asana has a very user-friendly UI. The collaboration tools are great, including app mentioning on specific tasks and messages. I feel that the workflow builder is not user friendly, and I struggle with the customization of the platform. Biggest challenge we have is permissioning. You know, making sure that our clients can't export our task list and share them with other organizations. I believe that that is a feature of our license, but it should be a feature across all different license offerings with Asana. Especially for smaller organizations, we cannot always afford the enterprise level subscription. So having more features in the basic subscription would be helpful. Asana helps me organize tasks and keeps our team aligned. I value its collaboration tools, task dependencies for project structuring, and portfolios for organizing work and reporting. Great UX for Project Planning, Scheduling, and Cross-Team Collaboration

title: Streamlined Project Tracking and Collaboration with a Clean, User-Friendly Interface
reviewText: What I like most about Asana is how straightforward it makes project tracking and team coordination. The interface is clean and user-friendly, so creating tasks, assigning responsibilities, setting deadlines, and keeping an eye on progress all feels efficient and manageable. The timeline and board views make it easy to visualize each project stage, and the notifications and reminders help ensure nothing slips through the cracks. It also strengthens collaboration since team members can comment directly on tasks, attach files, and update statuses in one central place. Overall, it saves time, improves accountability, and keeps work organized, even when I’m managing multiple projects at once. One thing I dislike about Asana is that some of the more advanced features are only available in the higher pricing plans, which can feel limiting for smaller teams. Also, when I’m managing multiple projects at once, the volume of notifications can become overwhelming. For new users, it may take time to fully understand everything that’s available, including features like rules, dependencies, and reporting. A simpler onboarding experience, along with more flexibility in the lower-tier plans, would make Asana even better. Asana helps solve the problem of scattered communication and missed deadlines by keeping tasks, updates, and responsibilities in one central place. Before using it, it was hard to track project progress across emails and chats, but now it’s much easier to assign work, monitor deadlines, and follow project status in real time. As a result, team accountability has improved, follow-up time has decreased, and overall project execution feels more organized and efficient—especially when managing multiple tasks at once. Streamlined Project Tracking and Collaboration with a Clean, User-Friendly Interface

title: Finest web-based collaboration and coordination tool
reviewText: Trello is a task management system that organizes projects around boards. In one glance, you can see what's being worked on, who's working on what, and where something is in a process.The card-based design makes it easy to visually understand your project, and the collaboration tools make it easy to share ideas with co-workers. The dependency on the web. I feel helpless if there is no internet connectivity. Trello works well for high-level project management, but it isn't a complete solution, so you'll need something else to manage your day-to-day tasks. The simplicity of Trello’s approach makes it scalable and flexible, from small businesses to large corporations. Any new user can easily understand the structure of trello and can create a board with lists and cards without any unnecessary chaos. Finest web-based collaboration and coordination tool

title: Finally a tool for collaborating with remote folks
reviewText: Trello is an amazing project management tool for distributed collaboration. What they have built into cards is brilliant (notes, attachments, to-do, voting). The key for me is how visual Trello is usability: - visual (color coding, swim lanes, avatars for ownership) - drag & drop, not clicks - very intuitive (new users can jump in after a super brief walk through) We really need story points for tracking which isn't included in the product. I was able to find a Chrome Extension (https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/scrum-for-trello/jdbcdblgjdpmfninkoogcfpnkjmndgje), but that doesn't help my co-workers who aren't using Chrome. Beats excel, email, google docs for personal of team project management. Give it a try. Finally a tool for collaborating with remote folks

title: Comprehensive, Easy-to-Use Tool for Dynamic Projects
reviewText: I find the kanban style interface of Trello is very clean and visual which in turn makes it easy to use and allowing me to see the state of a project immediately without a large learning process. As an engineer, the better automation is a game changer for me as it helps manage tasks efficiently. Trello's integration with Stack is excellent, keeping our team on the same page without in-depth manual updates, making it great for dynamic collaborative projects. The Kanban system and superior notification system are outstanding, along with its simple and affordable pricing. The adaptive design helps me a lot in my job. Setting up Trello was very easy for me, and I use it without any hassle. Honestly, Trello crumbles as soon as the project expands, as it becomes incredibly disorganised. In fact, there is no native integration with key Agile features, such as sprint planning and engineering ketrices, which means that tracking your own team or viewing burndown charts is out of that, being engineer, I find that the editor is very irritating. Trello makes it easy to use with a clean, visible interface. I can quickly assess project status, benefit from better automation, seamless GitHub integration for engineering tasks, and enjoy dynamic collaboration without manual updates. Comprehensive, Easy-to-Use Tool for Dynamic Projects

title: A project management solution characterized by its ease of use.
reviewText: Trello's ease of use is really impressive. All the dashboards are intuitive, so even an inexperienced user can quickly get their bearings and start creating visualizations. The drag-and-drop features make it easy to move boards and cards and keep the project on track. Personally, I like that Trello allows you to customize boards by changing labels, background color, etc. to quickly track progress. Of particular note is Butler, the Trello bot that allows you to create and customize automated actions such as moving boards and assigning tasks, which saves time and increases productivity. One of Trello's shortcomings is the lack of built-in reports and analytics, which are important to gain actionable insights. Unlike its competitors, which offer these features out of the box, Trello has to use extensions to get them. In addition, Trello may be of limited use for projects that require complex visualization and automation, as it was primarily designed for simple projects with few workflows. Compared to other project management software, the automation tools built into Trello are quite limited and I think this is an area where it could be improved. One of Trello's shortcomings is the lack of built-in reports and analytics, which are important to gain actionable insights. Unlike its competitors, which offer these features out of the box, Trello has to use extensions to get them. In addition, Trello may be of limited use for projects that require complex visualization and automation, as it was primarily designed for simple projects with few workflows. Compared to other project management software, the automation tools built into Trello are quite limited and I think this is an area where it could be improved. A project management solution characterized by its ease of use.

title: Visually appealing & easy to use, but limited
reviewText: The best thing about Trello is undoubtedly the visual appeal and ease of use. It's fairly customizable in terms of look/feel, and the drag-and-drop interface is simple to use and intuitive. I cannot stand that you're unable to actually "close out" tickets. All you can do is archive them but never truly mark them as complete. Keep in mind that this is a fairly light PM tool in terms of functionality and tracking things granularly or in "sprints." I would not recommend this for use with engineering teams, but for a marketing team such as my own, it does the job of keeping people organized. Everyone on my team was using their own method of project management, even on collaborative projects. This made it impossible to know what was going on across the team and the progress on joint efforts. Trello allowed us to all get on the same platform and use flags to differentiate between different sub-teams, and it was free! Visually appealing & easy to use, but limited

title: "Card"-based project management and collaboration
reviewText: Card-based project management. Track your tasks and ideas and events, etc., etc. into cards --> then sort those cards into lists! It's a great, intuitive way to do project management. Furthermore, cards can have templates and also can pack attachments, labels, members, comments, votes, etc! Doesn't work as well with Gantt charts or if you're running this MSProject-style This is Kanban (check it out) -style project management. It's completely free [forever] but may add pay-features in the future, so the price is definitely right! "Card"-based project management and collaboration

title: Our Go-To Tool for Content Management and Team Collaboration
reviewText: Trello’s visual simplicity and flexibility are what I like best. It’s easy to organize tasks, move them across stages, and collaborate with my team. Whether I’m working on blog content, YouTube videos, or other marketing projects, Trello’s intuitive drag-and-drop interface allows for smooth management. I also appreciate the wide range of power-ups and integrations, especially with Google Drive and Slack, which help streamline our workflows. The ability to automate tasks using Butler is another huge time-saver for our team. While Trello excels at simplicity, it can feel limited when managing more complex projects. Features like detailed reporting, time tracking, and analytics are missing or require third-party integrations. It would also be helpful to have better task dependencies or Gantt chart functionality natively. Although there are workarounds with power-ups, these features could improve Trello’s utility for teams managing larger projects or requiring more detailed project management capabilities. Trello makes it super easy to keep all our content projects organized in one place, which was a big problem before. We used to struggle with tracking progress and knowing who was responsible for what—especially when it came to managing blog posts, YouTube videos, and our overall content calendar. Now, everything’s laid out clearly on one dashboard, so the whole team can see what's going on. It's really improved how we communicate and work together. We don’t miss deadlines anymore, and we can adjust to changes without feeling overwhelmed. Plus, the automation tools like Butler save us a ton of time on repetitive stuff, so we can focus more on the actual content. Overall, it’s just made our workflow smoother and more efficient. Our Go-To Tool for Content Management and Team Collaboration

title: Trello - Manage projects in a very interactive interface.
reviewText: I really like its interface, all its tools load super fast, it allows me to create cards in which I can drag and drop my scheduled activities, I can assign these very easily to any member of the team. Another very good advantage is that it has a tool to create diagrams, reports and the possibility of installing plugins to send emails. The customization of the boards is too limited, I can only change the background image, the stickers are few, and the monthly cost is not fixed and varies depending on the number of people on the team. As for the additional plugins (Power-Ups), these are very helpful, however sometimes they do not offer many options and are very limited, it depends on the service you want to integrate. For my work team and the company, Trello has been a tool with which we have streamlined our work, since it allowed us to improve the monitoring of our pending activities, improved the workflow and also helps us generate reports of all the activities carried out. Trello - Manage projects in a very interactive interface.

title: Great project manager
reviewText: What I like a lot about Trello is that it is really easy to use. Performance is optimal, and the huge amount of free features that it provides make this application an awesome project manager. I used it both for personal and professional projects and it always helped me organise my tasks in a very intuitive and easy way. There is only one thing I miss, but it is quite specific and there is for sure a reason why this feature is not there. When talking about automation and rules, I would like to have as action the possibility to change the Cover of an added card. However, as I say, it is a very small and specific detail. I like to have my tasks in a single place and in a very organised way. And with tasks I mean personal stuff, work-related tasks, etcetera. With Trello, every time I have something in my head that I am afraid to forget, I create a new task so I can easily review it later. Trello has basically been a solution for my day-to-day duties for the last years. Great project manager

title: A powerful product management tool
reviewText: - Trello uses board layout which makes it easy for what we need to do and assign members responsible for that task. - Team members can work on card simultaneously. - Great customisation like changing color, background etc. - Integrates with many other tools and allows to connect it with existing software - Great free plan which is helpful for a small number of members. - There is no support of inbuilt time tracking or loggin feature. - For larger projects it can be complex to handle all the moving parts. - Lack of charts which creates problems for teams which have to track dependencies and schedules. - Working over tasks and keeping a checklist of items within the projects and collaborating with other team members in it. - Able to use Trello from mobile app as well so managing tasks on the fly is easy A powerful product management tool

title: Excellent organization tool
reviewText: Trello manages to captivate at first glance with its visually appealing approach. Its intuitive and easy-to-use interface is a godsend for anyone who wants to get up to speed quickly and efficiently. Trello's boards, lists and cards are the perfect canvas for capturing our ideas, sharing tasks and collaborating with colleagues, giving us a bird's eye view of our progress and goals. Another plus point is Trello's customizability, which allows us to adapt the system to the needs and preferences of each user. From the organization of personal projects to the management of work teams, Trello adapts to any situation and makes life easier for those who want to have total control over their activities. can become overwhelming for those who prefer a more linear and hierarchical structure in their organization. The flexibility and freedom that Trello offers can lead to confusion if clear rules of use and responsibilities are not established. As projects and tasks pile up, Trello can become chaotic if not managed properly. Too many cards and lists can lead to a loss of clarity and ultimately inefficiency. The biggest challenge Trello solves is the need to stay organized and focused in the hustle and bustle of everyday life. With its visual approach and ability to break down complex projects into manageable tasks, Trello allows for better time management and clear goals. Excellent organization tool

title: Excellent platform for organizing and managing your work tasks efficiently
reviewText: Trello is the most intuitive virtual board for managing tasks you'll find anywhere. It's free to use and its implementation is relatively simple, allowing you to create your board instantly. You can choose the template you want for your board depending on your needs and what you'll be using it for. For projects and daily work tasks, the default template will suffice. You're assigned three lists for your workspace: To Do, Doing, and Done. Under these three lists or columns, you can add cards, where you can add the title of what the task is and further details on what this task is supposed to accomplish. The cards are movable by dragging and dropping, so you can share the progress of your work by moving a card from To Do to Doing or Doing to Done effectively. This is essential when you're working on projects collaboratively with other people, where each person has their set of individual tasks. They can move the cards of the tasks they're assigned to the respective column to share their progress with the rest of the team to improve coordination, especially in instances where certain tasks rely on the completion of another. Trello also allows you to leave remarks under each card, where you can keep notes and reminders similar to sticky notes for everyone to see. Trello is pretty customizable, allowing you to create such lists and rename them to your preference. For every workspace or board you create, you can invite all members of your team to collaborate with you and manage the board and your projects better. There's no limit to how many workspaces you can create, so if you're working with more than one team or dividing your team into smaller units for specific areas of work, you can use multiple workspaces to avoid clutter. To make your work easier, Trello supports Power-Ups, where you can integrate and add additional features to your boards such as Google Drive and Dropbox for file management. Trello can also be integrated with Zoom, where you can access your board and screen share it during your meetings. Additionally, Trello has built-in automation enabled for all users to access. You can create rules that can automatically respond to deadlines and specific actions set by you. If you're working with a deadline or you're required to submit reports for the progress of your projects, you can use Trello's automation to set up email reports. Trello will send weekly summaries of all the cards and which ones were moved to give you a summarized report on how the project is going along. Trello isn't as user-friendly as it would seem. It takes a bit of time to get used to it, so it's not ideal for someone who wants to get started with their projects immediately using boards. But aside from the learning curve, Trello is excellent and there aren't any issues that I find with it. If you're looking for a free-to-use project management and collaboration software, look no further than Trello. It has a bit of a learning curve, but it's worth the usage once you get a hang of it as it allows easy task management along with your team. Trello is our go-to task management platform due to its simplicity and ease of tracking. We've been using it for years and during the pandemic, it's been an excellent medium for us to use our workspace to manage and carry out our project work remotely. We've added everyone from our team to our workspace and each card has a small note with their name, making it easier for us to know which task is picked up by who. We've sorted our work into lists of to-do, doing, and done to know where we are in terms of progress concerning our deadlines. It allows collaboration to be done easier and any authorized member can update the board to move the tasks to a different list to provide updates. There's also a small chat space that we can use to leave messages and reminders concerning our work. Trello's automation is time-saving when creating smart buttons and generating reports based on the changes made to the board. It automatically encapsulates all the card movements that we do to update the progress and summarizes them into a report that we can send to the higher-ups or clients to provide information on how far into the project we are to completion. The Power-Ups make it convenient for us to manage our Trello board from other applications such as Zoom and also connect cloud hosting apps like Dropbox to save our boards on the cloud and move files. Excellent platform for organizing and managing your work tasks efficiently

title: Perfect for organizing and streamlining our workflow.
reviewText: Trello's strength is that it is simple to use and has sophisticated functionality hidden behind the surface. Since being bought by Atlassian, the platform's capabilities has been increasing, which has been a beneficial shift. I enjoy how it boosts efficiency by organizing our whole workflow. I also enjoy how various the themes for Trello tickets are. It allows you to quickly and efficiently generate a Trello ticket for a specific support request that follows the same pattern. After utilizing Monday.com, several UI elements felt clumsy. For example, there is no option to designate activities as "done" with a single click by navigating to the "Done" column. Dragging them one by one becomes tedious after a while. On the board, the different rows should be more easily identified. Colors, backgrounds, and row headings should be bold and distinct. It might take me a bit to find the precise ticket I'm looking for. Do some research on Trello and check out all of the features it has to offer; it's a lot of fun and you'll be amazed at how handy it can be. Before looking at which tools will or will not help you to pick the proper tool, make sure you know how you want your team to operate. Obtaining team feedback, and consequently agreement and buy-in, is critical to adoption. We have tickets for various support requests that come into our office. Each request has a deadline, thus the expiry date that may be defined is useful. Team members kept distinct to-do lists in silos that didn't account for dependence on colleagues or give visibility into workload management. Trello has assisted me in enabling teams to better prioritize, unlock issues, and transition to an Agile Scrum style. It also assisted team members in focusing and having a clearer mind; no more dragging jobs or thoughts around in your head. Perfect for organizing and streamlining our workflow.

title: One of the best project management tool i ever used
reviewText: Lots of things I like about Trello, First of all, it's a web-based platform and works smoothly; Trello has some beautiful templates to get started instantly with your project or task management. You can go creative with Trello as it has lots of customization options such as adding cards to the board, changing themes, and so on. Highly recommend by me, Trello is one of the most valuable tools for daily business work. If you need something to keep track of your projects and team members with complete control, Trello is here for you. Trello is offering the most helpful feature that you will need to track and manage every single task of your business. We use Trello to manage and keep track of every project and task that we do daily. It's a great way to keep track of them and collaborate online with all the members. I do assign separate people for separate task cards, and it works like a charm. Card management is very easy too. Lots of customization options in Trello to make the workplace more beautiful and focused.teamwork in Trello is accurate and works flawlessly, I can also attach a document or file in the card so when I open I card to complete the task, I know what to do exactly and where to work on. It's just in one project and task management tool for me. One of the best project management tool i ever used

title: Great Tool For Scrum Team
reviewText: The fun elements like stickers make it more interesting for the team and initiate conversations. The free-flowing UI for moving tasks that do not follow rigid lines and sizes, making it more flexible. Love the ability to picture the actual progress on a single board with images, graphics, reaction all in one place. Integration to confluence and slack. I prefer more information on a task by mouse over rather than having to open each task, which is not possible with Trello. Would appreciate better integration with jira being from the same parent. If you are already in Atlassian environment, Trello is definitely worth a try. We use it for high-level project planning. It gives a perfect picture of the states of all epics that need to be worked on. It helps us manage workload better by assigning big-picture tasks to different sprints. My team also uses it for retrospectives where team can add cards for various pain points, what went well, what did not etc. Great Tool For Scrum Team
